subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write the principle of working of a transformer. With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the working of a step-up transformer.
  2. An ideal transformer is designed to convert 50 V into 250 V. It draws 200 W power from an ac source whose instantaneous voltage is given by {tex}{v}_{{i}}=20 \sin (100 \pi) {t} {V}{/tex}.
    Find:
    1. rms value of input current.
    2. expression for instantaneous output voltage.
    3. expression for instantaneous output current.
question_text: Write the principle of working of a transformer. With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the working of a step-up transformer. An ideal transformer is designed to convert 50 V into 250 V. It draws 200 W power from an ac source whose instantaneous voltage is given by {tex}{v}_{{i}}=20 \sin (100 \pi) {t} {V}{/tex} . Find: rms value of input current. expression for instantaneous output voltage. expression for instantaneous output current.
explanation: Principle : It works on the principle of mutual induction. Working: When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Since the no. of turns are more in secondary windings an emf induced is proportional to the no. of turns. Therefore more emf is developed across the secondary windings. {tex}P_{i}=V_{p} I_{p}{/tex} {tex}200=\frac{20}{\sqrt{2}} I_{p}{/tex} {tex}I_{p}=10 \sqrt{2} {~A}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{o}}{V_{i}}=\frac{250}{50}{/tex} {tex}5=\frac{V_{o}}{V_{i}}{/tex} {tex}V_{o}=100 \sin (100 \pi) {tV}{/tex} {tex}{P}_{o}=\left(V_{o}\right)_{r m s}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}{/tex} {tex}200=\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}{/tex} {tex}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}=2 \sqrt{2} {~A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I_{o} =(2 \sqrt{2}) \sqrt{2} \sin (100 \pi) {t}{/tex} {tex}I_{o} =4 \sin (100 \pi) {t} {~A}{/tex}
solution: Principle : It works on the principle of mutual induction. Working: When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Since the no. of turns are more in secondary windings an emf induced is proportional to the no. of turns. Therefore more emf is developed across the secondary windings. {tex}P_{i}=V_{p} I_{p}{/tex} {tex}200=\frac{20}{\sqrt{2}} I_{p}{/tex} {tex}I_{p}=10 \sqrt{2} {~A}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{o}}{V_{i}}=\frac{250}{50}{/tex} {tex}5=\frac{V_{o}}{V_{i}}{/tex} {tex}V_{o}=100 \sin (100 \pi) {tV}{/tex} {tex}{P}_{o}=\left(V_{o}\right)_{r m s}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}{/tex} {tex}200=\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}{/tex} {tex}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}=2 \sqrt{2} {~A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I_{o} =(2 \sqrt{2}) \sqrt{2} \sin (100 \pi) {t}{/tex} {tex}I_{o} =4 \sin (100 \pi) {t} {~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Principle: It works on the principle of mutual induction.

    Working: When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Since the no. of turns are more in secondary windings an emf induced is proportional to the no. of turns. Therefore more emf is developed across the secondary windings.
  2. {tex}P_{i}=V_{p} I_{p}{/tex}
    {tex}200=\frac{20}{\sqrt{2}} I_{p}{/tex}
    {tex}I_{p}=10 \sqrt{2} {~A}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_{o}}{V_{i}}=\frac{250}{50}{/tex}
    {tex}5=\frac{V_{o}}{V_{i}}{/tex}
    {tex}V_{o}=100 \sin (100 \pi) {tV}{/tex}
    {tex}{P}_{o}=\left(V_{o}\right)_{r m s}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}{/tex}
    {tex}200=\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}{/tex}
    {tex}\left(I_{o}\right)_{r m s}=2 \sqrt{2} {~A}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore I_{o} =(2 \sqrt{2}) \sqrt{2} \sin (100 \pi) {t}{/tex}
    {tex}I_{o} =4 \sin (100 \pi) {t} {~A}{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate between the peak value and root mean square value of an alternating current. Derive the expression for the root mean square value of alternating current, in terms of its peak value.
question_text: Differentiate between the peak value and root mean square value of an alternating current. Derive the expression for the root mean square value of alternating current, in terms of its peak value.
explanation: Peak value is the maximum value of the alternating current. rms current is the equivalent dc current that would produce the same average power loss as the alternating current. The instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor is {tex}P=i^2 R=i_m^2 R \sin ^2 \omega t{/tex} The average power over a cycle is:- {tex} \overline{{P}}=\left\langle{i}^2 {R}\right\rangle=\left\langle{i}_{{m}}^2 {R} \sin ^2 \omega {t}\right\rangle {/tex} {tex} \left\langle\sin ^2 \omega {t}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} \overline{{P}}=\frac{1}{2} {i}_{{m}}^2 {R}={I}_{{mas}}^2 R {/tex} {tex} {I}_{{ms}}=\sqrt{\frac{{i}_{{m}}^2}{2}}=\frac{{i}_{{m}}}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}
solution: Peak value is the maximum value of the alternating current. rms current is the equivalent dc current that would produce the same average power loss as the alternating current. The instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor is {tex}P=i^2 R=i_m^2 R \sin ^2 \omega t{/tex} The average power over a cycle is:- {tex} \overline{{P}}=\left\langle{i}^2 {R}\right\rangle=\left\langle{i}_{{m}}^2 {R} \sin ^2 \omega {t}\right\rangle {/tex} {tex} \left\langle\sin ^2 \omega {t}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex} \overline{{P}}=\frac{1}{2} {i}_{{m}}^2 {R}={I}_{{mas}}^2 R {/tex} {tex} {I}_{{ms}}=\sqrt{\frac{{i}_{{m}}^2}{2}}=\frac{{i}_{{m}}}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Peak value is the maximum value of the alternating current.
rms current is the equivalent dc current that would produce the same average power loss as the alternating current.
The instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor is {tex}P=i^2 R=i_m^2 R \sin ^2 \omega t{/tex}
The average power over a cycle is:-
{tex} \overline{{P}}=\left\langle{i}^2 {R}\right\rangle=\left\langle{i}_{{m}}^2 {R} \sin ^2 \omega {t}\right\rangle {/tex}
{tex} \left\langle\sin ^2 \omega {t}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \overline{{P}}=\frac{1}{2} {i}_{{m}}^2 {R}={I}_{{mas}}^2 R {/tex}
{tex} {I}_{{ms}}=\sqrt{\frac{{i}_{{m}}^2}{2}}=\frac{{i}_{{m}}}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}

question_text: A {tex}20 \mu {~F}{/tex} capacitor is connected to a {tex}220 {~V}, \frac{125}{\pi} {~Hz}{/tex} ac source. Find the capacitive reactance and the peak value of current in the circuit. If the frequency of the ac source is doubled, by what factor will the capacitive reactance and the current be changed?
explanation: {tex} X_c =\frac{1}{2 \pi v C} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times \frac{125}{\pi} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} {/tex} {tex} =200 \Omega {/tex} {tex} I_0 =\frac{V_0}{X_C} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{220 \times 1.414}{200} {/tex} {tex} =1.6 {~A} \text { or } 1.1 \sqrt{2} {~A} {/tex} {tex}{X}_{{c}}{/tex} becomes half Current becomes double.
solution: {tex} X_c =\frac{1}{2 \pi v C} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times \frac{125}{\pi} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} {/tex} {tex} =200 \Omega {/tex} {tex} I_0 =\frac{V_0}{X_C} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{220 \times 1.414}{200} {/tex} {tex} =1.6 {~A} \text { or } 1.1 \sqrt{2} {~A} {/tex} {tex}{X}_{{c}}{/tex} becomes half Current becomes double.

    {tex} =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times \frac{125}{\pi} \times 20 \times 10^{-6}} {/tex}
    {tex} =200 \Omega {/tex}
    {tex} I_0 =\frac{V_0}{X_C} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{220 \times 1.414}{200} {/tex}
    {tex} =1.6 {~A} \text { or } 1.1 \sqrt{2} {~A} {/tex}
  2. {tex}{X}_{{c}}{/tex} becomes half

subject: Physics
question_html: An inductor, a capacitor and a resistor are connected in series with an ac source {tex}{v}={v}_{{m}} \sin \omega t{/tex}. Derive an expression for the average power dissipated in the circuit. Also obtain the expression for the resonant frequency of the circuit.
question_text: An inductor, a capacitor and a resistor are connected in series with an ac source {tex}{v}={v}_{{m}} \sin \omega t{/tex} . Derive an expression for the average power dissipated in the circuit. Also obtain the expression for the resonant frequency of the circuit.
explanation: For a series RLC circuit with an AC source v = {tex}v_m \sin (\omega t){/tex} : Average Power Dissipated : The total impedance is Z = {tex}\sqrt{R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}{/tex} . The RMS current is {tex}I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{v_m / \sqrt{2}}{Z}{/tex} . Average dissipated in the resistor is: {tex}P=I_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 R=\frac{v_m^2 R}{2\left(R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right)}{/tex} Resonant Frequency: Resonance occurs when {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} . Thus, the resonant frequency is: {tex}f_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}{/tex}
solution: For a series RLC circuit with an AC source v = {tex}v_m \sin (\omega t){/tex} : Average Power Dissipated : The total impedance is Z = {tex}\sqrt{R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}{/tex} . The RMS current is {tex}I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{v_m / \sqrt{2}}{Z}{/tex} . Average dissipated in the resistor is: {tex}P=I_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 R=\frac{v_m^2 R}{2\left(R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right)}{/tex} Resonant Frequency: Resonance occurs when {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} . Thus, the resonant frequency is: {tex}f_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}{/tex}
solution_html:
For a series RLC circuit with an AC source v = {tex}v_m \sin (\omega t){/tex}:
Average Power Dissipated: The total impedance is Z ={tex}\sqrt{R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}{/tex}. The RMS current is {tex}I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{v_m / \sqrt{2}}{Z}{/tex}. Average dissipated in the resistor is:
{tex}P=I_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 R=\frac{v_m^2 R}{2\left(R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right)}{/tex}
Resonant Frequency: Resonance occurs when {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex}. Thus, the resonant frequency is:
{tex}f_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw a labelled diagram of a step-up transformer and describe its working principle. Explain any three causes for energy losses in a real transformer.
  2. A step-up transformer converts a low voltage into high voltage. Does it violate the principle of conservation of energy? Explain.
  3. A step-up transformer has 200 and 3000 turns in its primary and secondary coils respectively. The input voltage given to the primary coil is 90 V. Calculate:
    1. The output voltage across the secondary coil
    2. The current in the primary coil if the current in the
      secondary coil is 2.0 A.
question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of a step-up transformer and describe its working principle. Explain any three causes for energy losses in a real transformer. A step-up transformer converts a low voltage into high voltage. Does it violate the principle of conservation of energy? Explain. A step-up transformer has 200 and 3000 turns in its primary and secondary coils respectively. The input voltage given to the primary coil is 90 V. Calculate: The output voltage across the secondary coil The current in the primary coil if the current in the secondary coil is 2.0 A.
explanation: The working principle of transformer is mutual induction. When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Causes of energy losses Copper Losses (I 2 R Losses): Explanation: These losses occur due to the resistance of the wire in the coils. When current flows through the wire, some energy is dissipated as heat due to the resistance. Mitigation: Using thicker wires with lower resistance and cooling mechanisms can help reduce these losses. Core Losses (Iron Losses): Explanation: Core losses include hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. Hysteresis loss occurs due to the lagging of magnetic domains behind the changing magnetic field. Eddy current loss is caused by circulating currents induced within the core material due to the changing magnetic field. Mitigation: Using laminated silicon steel cores reduces eddy currents, and using materials with low hysteresis loss minimizes core losses. Leakage Flux: Explanation: Not all magnetic flux produced by the primary coil links with the secondary coil. Some flux "leaks" and does not contribute to energy transfer between coils, leading to inefficiency. Mitigation: Improving core design and using better core materials can help reduce leakage flux. A step-up transformer does not violate the conservation of energy. It increases voltage by decreasing current proportionally. The power input (low voltage, high current) equals the power output (high voltage, low current), ensuring energy conservation. The transformer merely transfers energy, with losses typically due to inefficiencies. {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_P}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex} {tex}V_s=\frac{N_s}{N_P} \times V_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 90{/tex} {tex}V_s=1350 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_P}{I_s}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex} {tex}I_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 2=30 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution: The working principle of transformer is mutual induction. When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Causes of energy losses Copper Losses (I 2 R Losses): Explanation: These losses occur due to the resistance of the wire in the coils. When current flows through the wire, some energy is dissipated as heat due to the resistance. Mitigation: Using thicker wires with lower resistance and cooling mechanisms can help reduce these losses. Core Losses (Iron Losses): Explanation: Core losses include hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. Hysteresis loss occurs due to the lagging of magnetic domains behind the changing magnetic field. Eddy current loss is caused by circulating currents induced within the core material due to the changing magnetic field. Mitigation: Using laminated silicon steel cores reduces eddy currents, and using materials with low hysteresis loss minimizes core losses. Leakage Flux: Explanation: Not all magnetic flux produced by the primary coil links with the secondary coil. Some flux "leaks" and does not contribute to energy transfer between coils, leading to inefficiency. Mitigation: Improving core design and using better core materials can help reduce leakage flux. A step-up transformer does not violate the conservation of energy. It increases voltage by decreasing current proportionally. The power input (low voltage, high current) equals the power output (high voltage, low current), ensuring energy conservation. The transformer merely transfers energy, with losses typically due to inefficiencies. {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_P}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex} {tex}V_s=\frac{N_s}{N_P} \times V_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 90{/tex} {tex}V_s=1350 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_P}{I_s}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex} {tex}I_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 2=30 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The working principle of transformer is mutual induction.
    When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it.
    Causes of energy losses
    1. Copper Losses (I2R Losses):
      Explanation: These losses occur due to the resistance of the wire in the coils. When current flows through the wire, some energy is dissipated as heat due to the resistance.
      Mitigation: Using thicker wires with lower resistance and cooling mechanisms can help reduce these losses.
    2. Core Losses (Iron Losses):
      Explanation: Core losses include hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. Hysteresis loss occurs due to the lagging of magnetic domains behind the changing magnetic field. Eddy current loss is caused by circulating currents induced within the core material due to the changing magnetic field.
      Mitigation: Using laminated silicon steel cores reduces eddy currents, and using materials with low hysteresis loss minimizes core losses.
    3. Leakage Flux:
      Explanation: Not all magnetic flux produced by the primary coil links with the secondary coil. Some flux "leaks" and does not contribute to energy transfer between coils, leading to inefficiency.
      Mitigation: Improving core design and using better core materials can help reduce leakage flux.
  2. A step-up transformer does not violate the conservation of energy. It increases voltage by decreasing current proportionally. The power input (low voltage, high current) equals the power output (high voltage, low current), ensuring energy conservation. The transformer merely transfers energy, with losses typically due to inefficiencies.
    1. {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_P}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex}
      {tex}V_s=\frac{N_s}{N_P} \times V_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 90{/tex}
      {tex}V_s=1350 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{I_P}{I_s}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex}
      {tex}I_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 2=30 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: The primary and secondary coils of a transformer have 500 turns and 5000 turns respectively. The primary coil is connected to an ac source of 220 V 50 Hz. The output across the secondary coil is:
question_text: The primary and secondary coils of a transformer have 500 turns and 5000 turns respectively. The primary coil is connected to an ac source of 220 V 50 Hz. The output across the secondary coil is:

options.explanation: In a transformer with 500 turns in the primary coil and 5000 turns in the secondary coil, the voltage transformation can be determined using the formula: {tex} V_s=V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}{/tex} Here, {tex}V_p{/tex} is {tex}220 {~V}, N_p{/tex} is 500 , and {tex}N_s{/tex} is 5000. Calculating the secondary voltage: {tex} V_s=220 {~V} \times \frac{5000}{500}=220 {~V} \times 10=2200 {~V}{/tex} The frequency remains unchanged at 50 Hz. Therefore, the output across the secondary coil is 2200 V 50 Hz.

explanation: In a transformer with 500 turns in the primary coil and 5000 turns in the secondary coil, the voltage transformation can be determined using the formula: {tex} V_s=V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}{/tex} Here, {tex}V_p{/tex} is {tex}220 {~V}, N_p{/tex} is 500 , and {tex}N_s{/tex} is 5000. Calculating the secondary voltage: {tex} V_s=220 {~V} \times \frac{5000}{500}=220 {~V} \times 10=2200 {~V}{/tex} The frequency remains unchanged at 50 Hz. Therefore, the output across the secondary coil is 2200 V 50 Hz.

question_text: Draw the graphs showing the variation of the following with the frequency of ac source in a circuit: Resistance Capacitive reactance Inductive reactance Can the voltage drop across the inductor or the capacitor in a series LCR circuit be greater than the applied voltage of the ac source? Justify your answer.
explanation: Resistance Capacitive reactance Inductive reactance Yes, the voltage drop across the inductor or the capacitor in a series LCR (inductor-capacitor-resistor) circuit can be greater than the applied voltage of the AC source. This phenomenon occurs due to the concept of reactive voltage and phase differences in an AC circuit.
solution: Resistance Capacitive reactance Inductive reactance Yes, the voltage drop across the inductor or the capacitor in a series LCR (inductor-capacitor-resistor) circuit can be greater than the applied voltage of the AC source. This phenomenon occurs due to the concept of reactive voltage and phase differences in an AC circuit.
solution_html:
1. Resistance
    2. Capacitive reactance
    3. Inductive reactance
  2. Yes, the voltage drop across the inductor or the capacitor in a series LCR (inductor-capacitor-resistor) circuit can be greater than the applied voltage of the AC source. This phenomenon occurs due to the concept of reactive voltage and phase differences in an AC circuit.

question_html:
1. Describe the construction and working of a transformer and hence obtain the relation for {tex}\left(\frac{v_{{s}}}{v_{{p}}}\right){/tex} in terms of number of turns of primary and secondary.
  2. Discuss four main causes of energy loss in a real transformer.
question_text: Describe the construction and working of a transformer and hence obtain the relation for {tex}\left(\frac{v_{{s}}}{v_{{p}}}\right){/tex} in terms of number of turns of primary and secondary. Discuss four main causes of energy loss in a real transformer.
explanation: Construction: A transformer consists of two sets of coils, insulated from each other. They are wound on a soft- iron core, either one on top of other or on separate limbs of the core. Alternatively Working: When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links with the secondary and induces an emf. in it. For an ideal transformer the induced emf {tex}\left(\varepsilon_{{p}}\right){/tex} in primary coil for applied alternating voltage ( {tex}{V}_{{P}}{/tex} ) {tex}\varepsilon_{{p}}={V}_{{P}}=-{N}_{{P}} \frac{{~d} \phi}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(1) e.m.f. induced {tex}\varepsilon_{{S}}{/tex} in the secondary coil {tex}\varepsilon_{{S}}={V}_{{S}}=-{N}_{{S}} \frac{{~d} \phi}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(2) From eq. (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}{/tex} Any four energy losses Flux leakage. Resistance of windings/ copper loss. Eddy currents/iron loss. Hysteresis. Magnetostriction.
solution: Construction: A transformer consists of two sets of coils, insulated from each other. They are wound on a soft- iron core, either one on top of other or on separate limbs of the core. Alternatively Working: When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links with the secondary and induces an emf. in it. For an ideal transformer the induced emf {tex}\left(\varepsilon_{{p}}\right){/tex} in primary coil for applied alternating voltage ( {tex}{V}_{{P}}{/tex} ) {tex}\varepsilon_{{p}}={V}_{{P}}=-{N}_{{P}} \frac{{~d} \phi}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(1) e.m.f. induced {tex}\varepsilon_{{S}}{/tex} in the secondary coil {tex}\varepsilon_{{S}}={V}_{{S}}=-{N}_{{S}} \frac{{~d} \phi}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(2) From eq. (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}{/tex} Any four energy losses Flux leakage. Resistance of windings/ copper loss. Eddy currents/iron loss. Hysteresis. Magnetostriction.
solution_html:
1. Construction: A transformer consists of two sets of coils, insulated from each other. They are wound on a soft- iron core, either one on top of other or on separate limbs of the core.
    Alternatively

    Working: When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links with the secondary and induces an emf. in it.
    For an ideal transformer the induced emf {tex}\left(\varepsilon_{{p}}\right){/tex} in primary coil for applied alternating voltage ({tex}{V}_{{P}}{/tex})
    {tex}\varepsilon_{{p}}={V}_{{P}}=-{N}_{{P}} \frac{{~d} \phi}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(1)
    e.m.f. induced {tex}\varepsilon_{{S}}{/tex} in the secondary coil
    {tex}\varepsilon_{{S}}={V}_{{S}}=-{N}_{{S}} \frac{{~d} \phi}{{dt}}{/tex} ...(2)
    From eq. (1) and (2)
    {tex}\frac{V_{S}}{V_{P}}=\frac{N_{S}}{N_{P}}{/tex}
  2. Any four energy losses
    1. Flux leakage.
    2. Resistance of windings/ copper loss.
    3. Eddy currents/iron loss.
    4. Hysteresis.
    5. Magnetostriction.

subject: Physics
question_html: A series combination of an inductor L, a capacitor C and a resistor R is connected across an ac source of voltage in a circuit. Obtain an expression for the average power consumed by the circuit. Find power factor for (i) purely inductive circuit, and (ii) purely resistive circuit.
question_text: A series combination of an inductor L, a capacitor C and a resistor R is connected across an ac source of voltage in a circuit. Obtain an expression for the average power consumed by the circuit. Find power factor for (i) purely inductive circuit, and (ii) purely resistive circuit.
explanation: Instantaneous Power is given by; P = VI = (V m sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\times{/tex} I m sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} P = {tex}\frac{V_m I_m}{2}{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos {tex}(2\omega t+\phi){/tex} ] ...(i) The average power over a cycle is given by the average of the two terms in the R.H.S of equation (i). It is only the second term which is time dependent .Its average is zero .Therefore, {tex}P_{a v g}=\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos \phi=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} P avg = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} Power factor for purely inductive circuit, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \phi=0{/tex} Power factor for purely resistive circuit; {tex}\phi=0 \Rightarrow \cos \phi=1{/tex}
solution: Instantaneous Power is given by; P = VI = (V m sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\times{/tex} I m sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} P = {tex}\frac{V_m I_m}{2}{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos {tex}(2\omega t+\phi){/tex} ] ...(i) The average power over a cycle is given by the average of the two terms in the R.H.S of equation (i). It is only the second term which is time dependent .Its average is zero .Therefore, {tex}P_{a v g}=\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos \phi=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} P avg = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} Power factor for purely inductive circuit, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \phi=0{/tex} Power factor for purely resistive circuit; {tex}\phi=0 \Rightarrow \cos \phi=1{/tex}
solution_html:
Instantaneous Power is given by;
P = VI = (Vm sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t) {tex}\times{/tex} Im sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}
P = {tex}\frac{V_m I_m}{2}{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos {tex}(2\omega t+\phi){/tex}] ...(i)
The average power over a cycle is given by the average of the two terms in the R.H.S of equation (i). It is only the second term which is time dependent .Its average is zero .Therefore,
{tex}P_{a v g}=\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos \phi=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
Pavg = Vrms Irms cos {tex}\phi{/tex}

  1. Power factor for purely inductive circuit, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \phi=0{/tex}
  2. Power factor for purely resistive circuit; {tex}\phi=0 \Rightarrow \cos \phi=1{/tex}

question_html: The primary coil having NP turns of an ideal transformer is supplied with an alternating voltage VP. Obtain an expression for the voltage VS induced in its secondary coil having NS turns. Mention two main sources of power loss in real transformers.
question_text: The primary coil having N P turns of an ideal transformer is supplied with an alternating voltage V P . Obtain an expression for the voltage V S induced in its secondary coil having N S turns. Mention two main sources of power loss in real transformers.
explanation: When alternating voltage V P is supplied to the primary coil, an emf is induced in this coil. {tex}\epsilon_p=-N_p \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} Induced emf in the secondary coil {tex}\epsilon_s=-N_s \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} If the current taken from the secondary coil is small i.e secondary is an open circuit than {tex}\epsilon_s=V_s{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_s=-N_s \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{p}}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} Two main sources of power loss Flux leakage Resistance of the windings
solution: When alternating voltage V P is supplied to the primary coil, an emf is induced in this coil. {tex}\epsilon_p=-N_p \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} Induced emf in the secondary coil {tex}\epsilon_s=-N_s \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} If the current taken from the secondary coil is small i.e secondary is an open circuit than {tex}\epsilon_s=V_s{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_s=-N_s \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{p}}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex} Two main sources of power loss Flux leakage Resistance of the windings
solution_html:
When alternating voltage VP is supplied to the primary coil, an emf is induced in this coil.
{tex}\epsilon_p=-N_p \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex}
Induced emf in the secondary coil
{tex}\epsilon_s=-N_s \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
If the current taken from the secondary coil is small i.e secondary is an open circuit than {tex}\epsilon_s=V_s{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{V}_s=-N_s \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{p}}} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex}
Two main sources of power loss
Flux leakage
Resistance of the windings

subject: Physics
question_html:
An alternating current I = 14 sin (100 {tex}\pi t{/tex}) A passes through a series combination of a resistor of 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an inductor of {tex}\left(\frac{2}{5 \pi}\right){/tex}H. Taking {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 1.4, calculate the

  1. rms value of the voltage drops across the resistor and the inductor, and
  2. power factor of the circuit.
question_text: An alternating current I = 14 sin (100 {tex}\pi t{/tex} ) A passes through a series combination of a resistor of 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an inductor of {tex}\left(\frac{2}{5 \pi}\right){/tex} H. Taking {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} = 1.4, calculate the rms value of the voltage drops across the resistor and the inductor, and power factor of the circuit.
explanation: I = 14 sin (100 {tex}\pi t{/tex} ) {tex}I_{r m s}=\frac{I_o}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{14}{1.4} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} I rms = 10 A R = 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Voltage drops across the resistor V R = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 300 V Voltage drops across the inductor {tex}X_L=\omega L=100 \pi \times \frac{2}{5 \pi}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore L=\frac{2}{5 \pi}\right]{/tex} V L = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} X L = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 400 V V L = 400 V {tex}Z=\sqrt{X_L^2+R^2}{/tex} {tex}Z=\sqrt{(40)^2+(30)^2}{/tex} = 50 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Cos {tex}\phi=\frac{30}{50}{/tex} Cos {tex}\phi=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
solution: I = 14 sin (100 {tex}\pi t{/tex} ) {tex}I_{r m s}=\frac{I_o}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{14}{1.4} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} I rms = 10 A R = 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Voltage drops across the resistor V R = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 300 V Voltage drops across the inductor {tex}X_L=\omega L=100 \pi \times \frac{2}{5 \pi}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore L=\frac{2}{5 \pi}\right]{/tex} V L = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} X L = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 400 V V L = 400 V {tex}Z=\sqrt{X_L^2+R^2}{/tex} {tex}Z=\sqrt{(40)^2+(30)^2}{/tex} = 50 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Cos {tex}\phi=\frac{30}{50}{/tex} Cos {tex}\phi=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
I = 14 sin (100 {tex}\pi t{/tex})
{tex}I_{r m s}=\frac{I_o}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{14}{1.4} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Irms = 10 A
R = 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Voltage drops across the resistor
VR = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 300 V
Voltage drops across the inductor
{tex}X_L=\omega L=100 \pi \times \frac{2}{5 \pi}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore L=\frac{2}{5 \pi}\right]{/tex}
VL = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} XL = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 400 V
VL = 400 V
{tex}Z=\sqrt{X_L^2+R^2}{/tex}
{tex}Z=\sqrt{(40)^2+(30)^2}{/tex}
= 50 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Cos {tex}\phi=\frac{30}{50}{/tex}
Cos {tex}\phi=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
The primary and the secondary coils of an ideal step-down transformer consist of 650 and 25 turns respectively. When the primary coil of this transformer is connected to 240 V mains, the current in the primary coil is 1.5 A. Calculate:

  1. the voltage across the secondary coil
  2. the current in the secondary coil
  3. the average power delivered to the output circuit
question_text: The primary and the secondary coils of an ideal step-down transformer consist of 650 and 25 turns respectively. When the primary coil of this transformer is connected to 240 V mains, the current in the primary coil is 1.5 A. Calculate: the voltage across the secondary coil the current in the secondary coil the average power delivered to the output circuit
explanation: N p = 650, N s = 25, V p = 240V, I p = 1.5A {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_p}=\frac{N_s}{N_p}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{N_s}{N_{\mathrm{n}}} \times \quad \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{25}{650} \times 240{/tex} {tex}=\frac{120}{13}{/tex} = 9.23 V {tex}\frac{N_s}{N_p}=\frac{I_p}{I_s}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{N_p \times I_p}{N_s}=\frac{650 \times 1.5}{25}=39 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} P av = V s I s {tex}=\frac{120}{13} \times{/tex} 39 = 360 W Hence the average power delivered to the output circuit is 360 W
solution: N p = 650, N s = 25, V p = 240V, I p = 1.5A {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_p}=\frac{N_s}{N_p}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{N_s}{N_{\mathrm{n}}} \times \quad \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{25}{650} \times 240{/tex} {tex}=\frac{120}{13}{/tex} = 9.23 V {tex}\frac{N_s}{N_p}=\frac{I_p}{I_s}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{N_p \times I_p}{N_s}=\frac{650 \times 1.5}{25}=39 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} P av = V s I s {tex}=\frac{120}{13} \times{/tex} 39 = 360 W Hence the average power delivered to the output circuit is 360 W
solution_html:
Np = 650, Ns = 25, Vp = 240V, Ip = 1.5A

  1. {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_p}=\frac{N_s}{N_p}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{N_s}{N_{\mathrm{n}}} \times \quad \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{25}{650} \times 240{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{120}{13}{/tex}
    = 9.23 V
  2. {tex}\frac{N_s}{N_p}=\frac{I_p}{I_s}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{N_p \times I_p}{N_s}=\frac{650 \times 1.5}{25}=39 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
  3. Pav = VsIs
    {tex}=\frac{120}{13} \times{/tex} 39 = 360 W
    Hence the average power delivered to the output circuit is 360 W

question_html:
1. With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the principle and working of an ac generator. Hence, obtain an expression for the instantaneous value of the emf generated.
  2. The coil of an ac generator consists of 100 turns of wire, each of area 0.5 m2. The resistance of the wire is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The coil is rotating in a magnetic field of 0.8 T perpendicular to its axis of rotation, at a constant angular speed of 60 radian per second. Calculate the maximum emf generated and power dissipated in the coil.
question_text: With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the principle and working of an ac generator. Hence, obtain an expression for the instantaneous value of the emf generated. The coil of an ac generator consists of 100 turns of wire, each of area 0.5 m 2 . The resistance of the wire is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The coil is rotating in a magnetic field of 0.8 T perpendicular to its axis of rotation, at a constant angular speed of 60 radian per second. Calculate the maximum emf generated and power dissipated in the coil.
explanation: Diagram Principle - It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Whenever there is a change in magnetic flux linked with a coil , an emf is induced in the coil. Working - When a rectangular coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux changes continuously which induces an emf and the direction of current changes periodically. {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega{/tex} = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 2400 V hence, the maximum emf generated will be 2400 V. Power dissipated, P = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{r m s}^2}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2400}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{100}{/tex} = 28.8 kW
solution: Diagram Principle - It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Whenever there is a change in magnetic flux linked with a coil , an emf is induced in the coil. Working - When a rectangular coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux changes continuously which induces an emf and the direction of current changes periodically. {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega{/tex} = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 2400 V hence, the maximum emf generated will be 2400 V. Power dissipated, P = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{r m s}^2}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2400}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{100}{/tex} = 28.8 kW
solution_html:
1. Diagram

    Principle - It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Whenever there is a change in magnetic flux linked with a coil , an emf is induced in the coil.
    Working - When a rectangular coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux changes continuously which induces an emf and the direction of current changes periodically.
    {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
  2. {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega{/tex}
    = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60
    = 2400 V
    hence, the maximum emf generated will be 2400 V. 
    Power dissipated, P = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{r m s}^2}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2400}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{100}{/tex}
    = 28.8 kW

question_html:
An ac source of emf V = V0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C. Deduce the expression for the current (I) flowing in it. Plot the graph of

  1. V vs. {tex}\omega{/tex}t, and
  2. I vs. {tex}\omega{/tex}t.
question_text: An ac source of emf V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C. Deduce the expression for the current (I) flowing in it. Plot the graph of V vs. {tex}\omega{/tex} t, and I vs. {tex}\omega{/tex} t.
explanation: Let an alternating voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} ....(i) is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C. At any instant, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to this applied ac voltage. V = Potential difference across the capacitor {tex}= \frac { q } { C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = CV or q = CV o sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t Again we know that, the current I = {tex}\frac { d q } { d t } = \omega C V _ { 0 } \cos \omega t {/tex} or I = {tex}\frac{V_{0}}{(1 / \omega C)} \cos \omega t{/tex} , {tex}\frac {1}{\omega C} {/tex} is the capacitive reactance offered by the capacitor. or {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ....(ii) where, {tex}I _ { 0 } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } {/tex}
solution: Let an alternating voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} ....(i) is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C. At any instant, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to this applied ac voltage. V = Potential difference across the capacitor {tex}= \frac { q } { C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = CV or q = CV o sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t Again we know that, the current I = {tex}\frac { d q } { d t } = \omega C V _ { 0 } \cos \omega t {/tex} or I = {tex}\frac{V_{0}}{(1 / \omega C)} \cos \omega t{/tex} , {tex}\frac {1}{\omega C} {/tex} is the capacitive reactance offered by the capacitor. or {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ....(ii) where, {tex}I _ { 0 } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } {/tex}
solution_html:
Let an alternating voltage, V = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} ....(i)
is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C. At any instant, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to this applied ac voltage.

V = Potential difference across the capacitor {tex}= \frac { q } { C }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = CV or q = CVo sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t   
Again we know that, the current I = {tex}\frac { d q } { d t } = \omega C V _ { 0 } \cos \omega t {/tex} or I = {tex}\frac{V_{0}}{(1 / \omega C)} \cos \omega t{/tex}, {tex}\frac {1}{\omega C} {/tex}is the capacitive reactance offered by the capacitor.
or {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}....(ii)
where, {tex}I _ { 0 } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A resistor R and an inductor L are connected in series to a source of voltage V = Vo sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t. The voltage is found to lead current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}4{/tex}. If the inductor is replaced by a capacitor C, the voltage lags behind current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}4{/tex}. When L, C and R are connected in series with the same source, Find the:

  1. average power dissipated and
  2. instantaneous current in the circuit.
question_text: A resistor R and an inductor L are connected in series to a source of voltage V = V o sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t. The voltage is found to lead current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}4{/tex} . If the inductor is replaced by a capacitor C, the voltage lags behind current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}4{/tex} . When L, C and R are connected in series with the same source, Find the: average power dissipated and instantaneous current in the circuit.
explanation: For LR circuit {tex}\frac{X_L}{R}{/tex} = tan 45 o = 1 So, X L = R For CR circuit {tex}\frac{X_C}{R}{/tex} = tan 45 o = 1 So, X c = R For LCR circuit When L, C and R are connected in series, then actually three R are connected in series. Thus circuit is resistive. V = V 0 sin {tex} \omega{/tex} t and I = I 0 sin {tex} \omega{/tex} t Average power dissipation, P = VI P = V 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t Over full cycle, P AVG = {tex}\frac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Instantaneous current, I = I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t
solution: For LR circuit {tex}\frac{X_L}{R}{/tex} = tan 45 o = 1 So, X L = R For CR circuit {tex}\frac{X_C}{R}{/tex} = tan 45 o = 1 So, X c = R For LCR circuit When L, C and R are connected in series, then actually three R are connected in series. Thus circuit is resistive. V = V 0 sin {tex} \omega{/tex} t and I = I 0 sin {tex} \omega{/tex} t Average power dissipation, P = VI P = V 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t Over full cycle, P AVG = {tex}\frac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Instantaneous current, I = I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t
solution_html:
For LR circuit
{tex}\frac{X_L}{R}{/tex} = tan 45o = 1
So, XL = R
For CR circuit
{tex}\frac{X_C}{R}{/tex} = tan 45o = 1
So, Xc = R
For LCR circuit
When L, C and R are connected in series, then actually three R are connected in series. Thus circuit is resistive.
V = V0 sin{tex} \omega{/tex}t
and I = I0 sin {tex} \omega{/tex}t

  1. Average power dissipation, P = VI
    P = V0sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t {tex}\times{/tex} I0sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t
    Over full cycle,
    PAVG = {tex}\frac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
  2. Instantaneous current, I = I0sin{tex}\omega{/tex}t

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain with the help of a diagram, the working of a step-down transformer. Why is a laminated iron core used in a transformer?
question_text: Explain with the help of a diagram, the working of a step-down transformer. Why is a laminated iron core used in a transformer?
explanation: Working of a step-down transformer: A step-down transformer converts a high voltage at the primary side to a low voltage at the secondary side. Transformer works on the principle of "Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction". The emf induced is determined by the number of turns in primary and secondary windings. This ratio is called as turns ratio (or transformation ratio). The voltage reduction capability of step-down transformers depends on the turns ratio of the primary and secondary coils. As the number of turns in secondary coil is kept less as compared to the number of turns in primary coil, the amount of flux linkage to the secondary coil of the transformer becomes less compared to the primary coil. Accordingly, the emf induced becomes less in the secondary coil. {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}=\frac{V_S}{V_p}{/tex} Here, N s = Number of turns in secondary coil N p = Number of turns in primary coil V s = Voltage in secondary coil V p = Voltage in primary coil The number of turns in secondary winding is always less than the number of turns in the primary winding of the transformer, i.e., Np > N s . As the number of turns is less in secondary coil, so the induced emf (output voltage) in the secondary coil is less than the primary input voltage. Laminated iron core is used in transformers to reduce the Eddy current which prevents the efficient transfer of energy from the primary coil to the secondary coil and causes energy loss in form of heat generation in the core.
solution: Working of a step-down transformer: A step-down transformer converts a high voltage at the primary side to a low voltage at the secondary side. Transformer works on the principle of "Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction". The emf induced is determined by the number of turns in primary and secondary windings. This ratio is called as turns ratio (or transformation ratio). The voltage reduction capability of step-down transformers depends on the turns ratio of the primary and secondary coils. As the number of turns in secondary coil is kept less as compared to the number of turns in primary coil, the amount of flux linkage to the secondary coil of the transformer becomes less compared to the primary coil. Accordingly, the emf induced becomes less in the secondary coil. {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}=\frac{V_S}{V_p}{/tex} Here, N s = Number of turns in secondary coil N p = Number of turns in primary coil V s = Voltage in secondary coil V p = Voltage in primary coil The number of turns in secondary winding is always less than the number of turns in the primary winding of the transformer, i.e., Np > N s . As the number of turns is less in secondary coil, so the induced emf (output voltage) in the secondary coil is less than the primary input voltage. Laminated iron core is used in transformers to reduce the Eddy current which prevents the efficient transfer of energy from the primary coil to the secondary coil and causes energy loss in form of heat generation in the core.
solution_html:
Working of a step-down transformer:
A step-down transformer converts a high voltage at the primary side to a low voltage at the secondary side.

Transformer works on the principle of "Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction".
The emf induced is determined by the number of turns in primary and secondary windings. This ratio is called as turns ratio (or transformation ratio).
The voltage reduction capability of step-down transformers depends on the turns ratio of the primary and secondary coils. As the number of turns in secondary coil is kept less as compared to the number of turns in primary coil, the amount of flux linkage to the secondary coil of the transformer becomes less compared to the primary coil. Accordingly, the emf induced becomes less in the secondary coil.
{tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}=\frac{V_S}{V_p}{/tex}
Here,
Ns = Number of turns in secondary coil
Np = Number of turns in primary coil
Vs = Voltage in secondary coil
Vp = Voltage in primary coil
The number of turns in secondary winding is always less than the number of turns in the primary winding of the transformer, i.e., Np > Ns.
As the number of turns is less in secondary coil, so the induced emf (output voltage) in the secondary coil is less than the primary input voltage.
Laminated iron core is used in transformers to reduce the Eddy current which prevents the efficient transfer of energy from the primary coil to the secondary coil and causes energy loss in form of heat generation in the core.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Show that an ideal inductor does not dissipate power in an ac circuit.
  2. The variation of inductive reactance (XL) of an inductor with the frequency (f) of the ac source of 100 V and variable frequency is shown in the fig.
    1. Calculate the self-inductance of the inductor.
    2. When this inductor is used in series with a capacitor of unknown value and a resistor of 10{tex}\Omega{/tex} at 300 s-1, maximum power dissipation occurs in the circuit. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor.
question_text: Show that an ideal inductor does not dissipate power in an ac circuit. The variation of inductive reactance (X L ) of an inductor with the frequency (f) of the ac source of 100 V and variable frequency is shown in the fig. Calculate the self-inductance of the inductor. When this inductor is used in series with a capacitor of unknown value and a resistor of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at 300 s -1 , maximum power dissipation occurs in the circuit. Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor.
explanation: Power dissipation = P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{Z}{/tex} For ideal inductor R = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 Thus, ideal inductor does not dissipate power in an ac circuit. Inductive reactance = X L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex} From graph, at f = 100 Hz X L = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{2 \pi \times 100}{/tex} = 0.032 H = 32 mH Power dissipation is maximum when 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex} f = 300 s -1 L = 0.032 H 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex} Or, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.032 = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 300 \times C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 8.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F = 8.8 {tex}\mu{/tex} F
solution: Power dissipation = P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{Z}{/tex} For ideal inductor R = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 Thus, ideal inductor does not dissipate power in an ac circuit. Inductive reactance = X L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex} From graph, at f = 100 Hz X L = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{2 \pi \times 100}{/tex} = 0.032 H = 32 mH Power dissipation is maximum when 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex} f = 300 s -1 L = 0.032 H 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex} Or, 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.032 = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 300 \times C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 8.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F = 8.8 {tex}\mu{/tex} F
solution_html:
1. Power dissipation = P = Vrms Irms cos {tex}\phi{/tex}
    cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{Z}{/tex}
    For ideal inductor R = 0
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} P = Vrms Irms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0
    Thus, ideal inductor does not dissipate power in an ac circuit.
    1. Inductive reactance = XL = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}fL
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex}
      From graph, at f = 100 Hz
      XL = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{2 \pi \times 100}{/tex}
      = 0.032 H = 32 mH
    2. Power dissipation is maximum when
      2{tex}\pi{/tex}fL = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex}
      f = 300 s-1
      L = 0.032 H
      2{tex}\pi{/tex}fL = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex}
      Or, 2{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.032 = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 300 \times C}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 8.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F = 8.8 {tex}\mu{/tex}F

question_text: The figure shows the graphical variation of the reactance of a capacitor with frequency of ac source. Find the capacitance of the capacitor. An ideal inductor has the same reactance at 100 Hz frequency as the capacitor has at the same frequency. Find the value of inductance of the inductor. Draw the graph showing the variation of the reactance of this inductor with frequency.
explanation: From graph Xc = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at v = 100 Hz X c = {tex}\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi U C}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi v X_c}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 600}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1}{1200 \pi}{/tex} = 0.265mF = 0.265 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 f X c = X L = {tex}\omega{/tex} L = 6 at 100 Hz L = {tex}\frac{6}{2 \pi v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{2 \pi \times 100}{/tex} = 0.955 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 H hence, the value of inductance of the inductor will be 0.955 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 H.
solution: From graph Xc = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at v = 100 Hz X c = {tex}\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi U C}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi v X_c}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 600}{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{1}{1200 \pi}{/tex} = 0.265mF = 0.265 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 f X c = X L = {tex}\omega{/tex} L = 6 at 100 Hz L = {tex}\frac{6}{2 \pi v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{2 \pi \times 100}{/tex} = 0.955 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 H hence, the value of inductance of the inductor will be 0.955 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 H.
solution_html:
1. From graph Xc = 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} at v = 100 Hz
    Xc = {tex}\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi U C}{/tex}
    C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi v X_c}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 600}{/tex}
    C = {tex}\frac{1}{1200 \pi}{/tex} = 0.265mF = 0.265 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−3f
  2. Xc = XL = {tex}\omega{/tex}L = 6 at 100 Hz
    L = {tex}\frac{6}{2 \pi v}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6}{2 \pi \times 100}{/tex} = 0.955 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2H
    hence, the value of inductance of the inductor will be 0.955 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2H.

question_html:
1. What is the principle of the transformer?
  2. Explain how laminating the core a transformer helps to reduce eddy current losses in it?
  3. Why the primary and secondary coils of a transformer are preferably wound on the same core?
question_text: What is the principle of the transformer? Explain how laminating the core a transformer helps to reduce eddy current losses in it? Why the primary and secondary coils of a transformer are preferably wound on the same core?
explanation: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction between the two circuits which is linked by a common magnetic flux. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). Laminations are thin, making the resistance higher. Eddy currents are confined within each thin lamination. This reduces the net eddy current. For maximum sharing of magnetic flux and magnetic flux per turn to be the same in both primary and secondary.
solution: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction between the two circuits which is linked by a common magnetic flux. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). Laminations are thin, making the resistance higher. Eddy currents are confined within each thin lamination. This reduces the net eddy current. For maximum sharing of magnetic flux and magnetic flux per turn to be the same in both primary and secondary.
solution_html:
1. A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction between the two circuits which is linked by a common magnetic flux. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil).
  2. Laminations are thin, making the resistance higher. Eddy currents are confined within each thin lamination. This reduces the net eddy current.
  3. For maximum sharing of magnetic flux and magnetic flux per turn to be the same in both primary and secondary.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Draw the diagram of a device which is used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage and state its working principle. Write four sources of energy loss in this device.
  2. A small town with a demand of 1200 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 20 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.5{tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets the power from the line through a 4000-220 V step-down transformer at a sub-station in the town. Estimate the line power loss in the form of heat
question_text: Draw the diagram of a device which is used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage and state its working principle. Write four sources of energy loss in this device. A small town with a demand of 1200 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 20 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets the power from the line through a 4000-220 V step-down transformer at a sub-station in the town. Estimate the line power loss in the form of heat
explanation: The device used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage is called transformer (step-down transformer). Working principle: Transformer works on the principle of Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction. The law of electromagnetic induction states that when magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the coil. Transformer consists of two coils called primary coil and secondary coil. The ac current in primary coil changes magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil and thus an emf is induced in the secondary coil. Sources of energy loss in transformer Copper loss: The coils of transformer (made of copper) have a finite resistance due to which some energy in lost as heat. Iron loss: Due to induced eddy currents in the iron core, some energy is lost in the bulk. Magnetic loss: Since all magnetic flux in primary coil does not pass through the secondary coil, there is some loss of energy due to leakage of flux. Hysteresis loss: alternating magnetization and demagnetization of the iron core cause some loss of energy in form of heat Demand of electric power = 1200 kW Distance of town from power station = 20 km Two wire = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 40 km Total resistance of line = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 200 The town gets a power of 4000 volts Power = voltage {tex}\times{/tex} current {tex}I=\frac{1200 \times 10^3}{4000}=\frac{1200}{4}=300 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} The line power loss in the form of heat = I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = (300) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 9000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1800 kW
solution: The device used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage is called transformer (step-down transformer). Working principle: Transformer works on the principle of Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction. The law of electromagnetic induction states that when magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the coil. Transformer consists of two coils called primary coil and secondary coil. The ac current in primary coil changes magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil and thus an emf is induced in the secondary coil. Sources of energy loss in transformer Copper loss: The coils of transformer (made of copper) have a finite resistance due to which some energy in lost as heat. Iron loss: Due to induced eddy currents in the iron core, some energy is lost in the bulk. Magnetic loss: Since all magnetic flux in primary coil does not pass through the secondary coil, there is some loss of energy due to leakage of flux. Hysteresis loss: alternating magnetization and demagnetization of the iron core cause some loss of energy in form of heat Demand of electric power = 1200 kW Distance of town from power station = 20 km Two wire = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 40 km Total resistance of line = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 200 The town gets a power of 4000 volts Power = voltage {tex}\times{/tex} current {tex}I=\frac{1200 \times 10^3}{4000}=\frac{1200}{4}=300 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} The line power loss in the form of heat = I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = (300) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 9000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1800 kW
solution_html:
1. The device used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage is called transformer (step-down transformer).
    Working principle:
    Transformer works on the principle of Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction. The law of electromagnetic induction states that when magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the coil. Transformer consists of two coils called primary coil and secondary coil. The ac current in primary coil changes magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil and thus an emf is induced in the secondary coil.

    Sources of energy loss in transformer
    1. Copper loss: The coils of transformer (made of copper) have a finite resistance due to which some energy in lost as heat. 
    2. Iron loss: Due to induced eddy currents in the iron core, some energy is lost in the bulk. 
    3. Magnetic loss: Since all magnetic flux in primary coil does not pass through the secondary coil, there is some loss of energy due to leakage of flux. 
    4. Hysteresis loss: alternating magnetization and demagnetization of the iron core cause some loss of energy in form of heat
  2. Demand of electric power = 1200 kW
    Distance of town from power station = 20 km Two wire = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 40 km
    Total resistance of line = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 200
    The town gets a power of 4000 volts
    Power = voltage {tex}\times{/tex} current
    {tex}I=\frac{1200 \times 10^3}{4000}=\frac{1200}{4}=300 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    The line power loss in the form of heat = I2 {tex}\times{/tex} R
    = (300)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
    = 9000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1800 kW

question_html:
A voltage v = vm sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t applied to a series LCR circuit, drives a current in the circuit given i = im sin ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex}). Deduce the expression for the instantaneous power supplied by the source. Hence, obtain the expression for the average power.
Define the terms power factor and wattless current, giving the examples where power factor is maximum and the circuit where there is wattless current.
question_text: A voltage v = v m sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t applied to a series LCR circuit, drives a current in the circuit given i = i m sin ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ). Deduce the expression for the instantaneous power supplied by the source. Hence, obtain the expression for the average power. Define the terms power factor and wattless current , giving the examples where power factor is maximum and the circuit where there is wattless current.
explanation: P i = V m sin wtI m sin(wt + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = V m I m sin wt[sin wt cos {tex}\phi{/tex} + cos wt sin {tex}\phi{/tex} ] {tex}P=V I \sin ^2 w t \cos \phi+\frac{2 V_m I_m}{2} \sin w t \cos w t \sin \phi{/tex} {tex}V_m I_m \sin w t \cos \phi+\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \sin 2 w t \sin \phi{/tex} {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{\int_o^T P_i d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{T}\left[V_m I_m \int_0^T\left(\frac{1-\cos \phi}{2}\right) \cos \phi d t+\int_o^T V_m I_m \frac{\sin 2 w t \sin \phi d t}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{V_m I_m \cos \phi}{2 T} \int_o^T d t=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} X \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} Power factor- cos {tex}\phi{/tex} factor in P av is power factor. Wattless Current- A current which has a phase difference of {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex} with voltage is called wattless current as there is no dissipation of power by this current. Current in purely inductive or purely capacitive circuit is wattless.
solution: P i = V m sin wtI m sin(wt + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = V m I m sin wt[sin wt cos {tex}\phi{/tex} + cos wt sin {tex}\phi{/tex} ] {tex}P=V I \sin ^2 w t \cos \phi+\frac{2 V_m I_m}{2} \sin w t \cos w t \sin \phi{/tex} {tex}V_m I_m \sin w t \cos \phi+\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \sin 2 w t \sin \phi{/tex} {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{\int_o^T P_i d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{T}\left[V_m I_m \int_0^T\left(\frac{1-\cos \phi}{2}\right) \cos \phi d t+\int_o^T V_m I_m \frac{\sin 2 w t \sin \phi d t}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{V_m I_m \cos \phi}{2 T} \int_o^T d t=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} X \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} Power factor- cos {tex}\phi{/tex} factor in P av is power factor. Wattless Current- A current which has a phase difference of {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex} with voltage is called wattless current as there is no dissipation of power by this current. Current in purely inductive or purely capacitive circuit is wattless.
solution_html:
Pi = Vm sin wtIm sin(wt + {tex}\phi{/tex})
= VmIm sin wt[sin wt cos {tex}\phi{/tex} + cos wt sin {tex}\phi{/tex}]
{tex}P=V I \sin ^2 w t \cos \phi+\frac{2 V_m I_m}{2} \sin w t \cos w t \sin \phi{/tex}
{tex}V_m I_m \sin w t \cos \phi+\frac{V_m I_m}{2} \sin 2 w t \sin \phi{/tex}
{tex}P_{a v}=\frac{\int_o^T P_i d t}{T}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{T}\left[V_m I_m \int_0^T\left(\frac{1-\cos \phi}{2}\right) \cos \phi d t+\int_o^T V_m I_m \frac{\sin 2 w t \sin \phi d t}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}P_{a v}=\frac{V_m I_m \cos \phi}{2 T} \int_o^T d t=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} X \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
Power factor- cos{tex}\phi{/tex} factor in Pav is power factor.
Wattless Current- A current which has a phase difference of {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex} with voltage is called wattless current as there is no dissipation of power by this current.
Current in purely inductive or purely capacitive circuit is wattless.

question_html:
Given three elements X, Y and Z to be connected across an ac source. With only X connected across the ac source, voltage and current are found to be in the same phase. With only element Y in the circuit, the voltage lags behind the the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, while with the element Z in the circuit, the voltage leads the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.

  1. Identify the elements X, Y and Z.
  2. When all these elements are connected in series across the same source, (i) determine the power factor, and (ii) find out the condition when the circuit is in resonant state.
question_text: Given three elements X, Y and Z to be connected across an ac source. With only X connected across the ac source, voltage and current are found to be in the same phase. With only element Y in the circuit, the voltage lags behind the the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , while with the element Z in the circuit, the voltage leads the current in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Identify the elements X, Y and Z. When all these elements are connected in series across the same source, (i) determine the power factor, and (ii) find out the condition when the circuit is in resonant state.
explanation: X is resistor Y is a capacitor Z is an inductor Power Factor {tex}=\frac{V_R}{V}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{Z}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}{/tex} Circuit is in resonance when X L - X C = 0 Alternatively {tex}\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega L}}{/tex}
solution: X is resistor Y is a capacitor Z is an inductor Power Factor {tex}=\frac{V_R}{V}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{Z}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}{/tex} Circuit is in resonance when X L - X C = 0 Alternatively {tex}\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega L}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. X is resistor
    Y is a capacitor
    Z is an inductor

    1. Power Factor {tex}=\frac{V_R}{V}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{R}{Z}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}{/tex}
    2. Circuit is in resonance when
      XL - XC = 0
      Alternatively
      {tex}\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega L}}{/tex}

question_html: Two electric bulbs P and Q have their resistances in the ratio of 1 : 2. They are connected in series across a battery. Find the ratio of the power dissipation in these bulbs.
question_text: Two electric bulbs P and Q have their resistances in the ratio of 1 : 2. They are connected in series across a battery. Find the ratio of the power dissipation in these bulbs.
explanation: Given: {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} We know that Dissipated Power, P = I 2 R As the batteries are connected in series, current is same in bulb P and bulb Q. Thus, {tex}\frac{P_{P}}{P_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{I^2R_{P}}{I^2R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} We know that Dissipated Power, P = I 2 R As the batteries are connected in series, current is same in bulb P and bulb Q. Thus, {tex}\frac{P_{P}}{P_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{I^2R_{P}}{I^2R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
We know that Dissipated Power, P = I2R
As the batteries are connected in series, current is same in bulb P and bulb Q.
Thus,
{tex}\frac{P_{P}}{P_{Q}}{/tex} =  {tex}\frac{I^2R_{P}}{I^2R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R_{P}}{R_{Q}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_text: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value 10 V is applied to a series LCR circuit in which resistance, capacitance, and inductance have values of 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F and 1 H respectively. Find the peak voltage across the inductor at resonance quality factor of the circuit.
explanation: I 0 = V 0 /R = 10/10 = 1 A ω r = 1/√LC = 1/√ (1 x 1 x 10 -6 ) = 10 3 rad/s V 0 = I 0 X L = I 0 ωr L = 1 x 10 3 x 1 = 10 3 V Q = ω r L/R = (10 3 x 1)/10 = 100
solution: I 0 = V 0 /R = 10/10 = 1 A ω r = 1/√LC = 1/√ (1 x 1 x 10 -6 ) = 10 3 rad/s V 0 = I 0 X L = I 0 ωr L = 1 x 10 3 x 1 = 10 3 V Q = ω r L/R = (10 3 x 1)/10 = 100
solution_html:
1. I0 = V0/R
    = 10/10
    = 1 A 
    ωr = 1/√LC = 1/√ (1 x 1 x 10-6)
    = 103 rad/s
    V0 = I0 XL = I0 ωr L = 1 x 103 x 1 = 103 V
  2. Q = ωr L/R
    = (103 x 1)/10 = 100

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Name the device used to change the alternating voltage to a higher or lower value. State one cause for power dissipation in this device.
  2. Explain with an example, how power loss is reduced if the energy is transmitted over long distances as an alternating current rather than a direct current.
question_text: Name the device used to change the alternating voltage to a higher or lower value. State one cause for power dissipation in this device. Explain with an example, how power loss is reduced if the energy is transmitted over long distances as an alternating current rather than a direct current.
explanation: Transformer is used to change the alternating voltage to a higher or lower value. One cause of power dissipation in transformer is the resistance (I 2 R loss) of the copper wire used for winding. At the generation end, the alternating voltage is stepped up by using a step-up transformer. So, the corresponding current reduces. It is transmitted through transmission lines. So, there is no appreciable I 2 R loss. At the receiving point, the voltage is stepped down to the desired level by using a step-down transformer and then it is distributed to consumers. This step-up and step down are not so easy processes for direct current.
solution: Transformer is used to change the alternating voltage to a higher or lower value. One cause of power dissipation in transformer is the resistance (I 2 R loss) of the copper wire used for winding. At the generation end, the alternating voltage is stepped up by using a step-up transformer. So, the corresponding current reduces. It is transmitted through transmission lines. So, there is no appreciable I 2 R loss. At the receiving point, the voltage is stepped down to the desired level by using a step-down transformer and then it is distributed to consumers. This step-up and step down are not so easy processes for direct current.
solution_html:
1. Transformer is used to change the alternating voltage to a higher or lower value. One cause of power dissipation in transformer is the resistance (I2R loss) of the copper wire used for winding.
  2. At the generation end, the alternating voltage is stepped up by using a step-up transformer. So, the corresponding current reduces. It is transmitted through transmission lines. So, there is no appreciable I2R loss. At the receiving point, the voltage is stepped down to the desired level by using a step-down transformer and then it is distributed to consumers.
    This step-up and step down are not so easy processes for direct current.

question_html:
1. State the principle of working of a transformer.
  2. Define the efficiency of a transformer.
  3. State any two factors that reduce the efficiency of a transformer.
  4. Calculate the current drawn by the primary of a 90% efficient transformer which steps down 220 V to 22 V if the output resistance is 440 {tex}\Omega{/tex}.
question_text: State the principle of working of a transformer. Define the efficiency of a transformer. State any two factors that reduce the efficiency of a transformer. Calculate the current drawn by the primary of a 90% efficient transformer which steps down 220 V to 22 V if the output resistance is 440 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
explanation: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). The efficiency of a transformer equals the ratio of the output power to the input power. The input and output power are measured in the same unit Alternatively: Efficiency = {tex}\frac{\text { output power }}{\text { input power }}{/tex} Efficiency = {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} Efficiency is affected by the factors Eddy current losses joule heat losses hysteresis losses magnetic flux leakage losses Calculating the current drawn We have {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} = 90% = 0.9 {tex}\therefore \frac{22 I_{s}}{220 I_{p}}{/tex} = 0.9 or {tex}\frac{I_{s}}{I_{p}}=\frac{0.9}{0.1}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I p = {tex}\frac{I_{5}}{9}=\frac{\left(\frac{22}{440}\right)}{9}{/tex} A {tex}=\frac{1}{180}{/tex} A = 0.0056 A
solution: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). The efficiency of a transformer equals the ratio of the output power to the input power. The input and output power are measured in the same unit Alternatively: Efficiency = {tex}\frac{\text { output power }}{\text { input power }}{/tex} Efficiency = {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} Efficiency is affected by the factors Eddy current losses joule heat losses hysteresis losses magnetic flux leakage losses Calculating the current drawn We have {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} = 90% = 0.9 {tex}\therefore \frac{22 I_{s}}{220 I_{p}}{/tex} = 0.9 or {tex}\frac{I_{s}}{I_{p}}=\frac{0.9}{0.1}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I p = {tex}\frac{I_{5}}{9}=\frac{\left(\frac{22}{440}\right)}{9}{/tex} A {tex}=\frac{1}{180}{/tex} A = 0.0056 A
solution_html:
1. A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction.
    (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil).
  2. The efficiency of a transformer equals the ratio of the output power to the input power. The input and output power are measured in the same unit
    Alternatively:
    Efficiency = {tex}\frac{\text { output power }}{\text { input power }}{/tex}
    Efficiency  = {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex}
  3. Efficiency is affected by the factors
    1. Eddy current losses
    2. joule heat losses
    3. hysteresis losses
    4. magnetic flux leakage losses
  4. Calculating the current drawn
    We have
    {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} = 90% = 0.9
    {tex}\therefore \frac{22 I_{s}}{220 I_{p}}{/tex} = 0.9
    or {tex}\frac{I_{s}}{I_{p}}=\frac{0.9}{0.1}{/tex} = 9
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Ip = {tex}\frac{I_{5}}{9}=\frac{\left(\frac{22}{440}\right)}{9}{/tex} A
    {tex}=\frac{1}{180}{/tex} A
    = 0.0056 A

question_html: With the help of a diagram, explain the principle of a device which changes a low voltage into a high voltage but does not violate the law of conservation of energy. Give any one reason why the device may not be 100% efficient.
question_text: With the help of a diagram, explain the principle of a device which changes a low voltage into a high voltage but does not violate the law of conservation of energy. Give any one reason why the device may not be 100% efficient.
explanation: Device - transformer {tex}\varepsilon_p{/tex} = -N p {tex}\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} ...(i) and emf induced in the secondary coil = -N S {tex}\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_S}{\varepsilon_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\frac{V_S}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_S}{\varepsilon_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}{/tex} = r (say) ...(iv) V p i p = V S i S {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{i_S}{i_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_p}{V_S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_p}{N_S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} ...(v) In step up transformer , N s > N p {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} r > 1; So V S > V p and i S < i p i.e., step up transformer increases the voltage. Two coils on separate limbs of the core Principle: It is based on the principle of mutual inductance and transforms the alternating low voltage to alternating high voltage and in this the number of turns in secondary coil is more than that in primary coil. (i.e., N S > Np). Efficiency: Assuming no energy losses, the transformer is 100% efficient i.e. I P V P = I s V s
solution: Device - transformer {tex}\varepsilon_p{/tex} = -N p {tex}\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} ...(i) and emf induced in the secondary coil = -N S {tex}\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_S}{\varepsilon_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\frac{V_S}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_S}{\varepsilon_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}{/tex} = r (say) ...(iv) V p i p = V S i S {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{i_S}{i_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_p}{V_S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_p}{N_S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} ...(v) In step up transformer , N s > N p {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} r > 1; So V S > V p and i S < i p i.e., step up transformer increases the voltage. Two coils on separate limbs of the core Principle: It is based on the principle of mutual inductance and transforms the alternating low voltage to alternating high voltage and in this the number of turns in secondary coil is more than that in primary coil. (i.e., N S > Np). Efficiency: Assuming no energy losses, the transformer is 100% efficient i.e. I P V P = I s V s
solution_html:
Device - transformer
{tex}\varepsilon_p{/tex} = -Np {tex}\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} ...(i)
and emf induced in the secondary coil
= -NS {tex}\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta t}{/tex} ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{\varepsilon_S}{\varepsilon_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\frac{V_S}{V_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_S}{\varepsilon_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_S}{N_p}{/tex} = r (say) ...(iv)
Vpip = VS iS
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{i_S}{i_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_p}{V_S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{N_p}{N_S}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} ...(v)
In step up transformer, Ns > Np {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} r > 1;
So VS > Vp and iS < ip
i.e., step up transformer increases the voltage.

Two coils on separate limbs of the core
Principle: It is based on the principle of mutual inductance and transforms the alternating low voltage to alternating high voltage and in this the number of turns in secondary coil is more than that in primary coil. (i.e., NS > Np).
Efficiency: Assuming no energy losses, the transformer is 100% efficient i.e. IPVP = IsVs
