question_html: When an a.c. source is connected to an ideal inductor, show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
question_text: When an a.c. source is connected to an ideal inductor, show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
explanation: As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex} In an ideal inductor, rms current I rms lags behind the applied rms voltage V rms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex} or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex}
solution: As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex} In an ideal inductor, rms current I rms lags behind the applied rms voltage V rms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex} or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex}
In an ideal inductor, rms current Irms lags behind the applied rms voltage Vrms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex}
or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: When an ideal capacitor is connected to an a.c. source, show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is Zero.
question_text: When an ideal capacitor is connected to an a.c. source, show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is Zero.
explanation: Average power associated with a capacitor: When an a.c. is applied to a capacitor, the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} radian. So we write the expressions for instantaneous voltage and current as follows: {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex} Work done in the circuit in small time dt will be dW = Pdt = VIdt = V 0 I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t dt {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} The average power dissipated per cycle in the capacitor is Pav = {tex}\frac{W}{T}=\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} d W=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 \omega t}{2 \omega}\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}\left[\cos \frac{4 \pi}{T} t\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[1-1]=0{/tex} Thus the average power dissipated per cycle in a capacitor is zero.
solution: Average power associated with a capacitor: When an a.c. is applied to a capacitor, the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} radian. So we write the expressions for instantaneous voltage and current as follows: {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex} Work done in the circuit in small time dt will be dW = Pdt = VIdt = V 0 I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t dt {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} The average power dissipated per cycle in the capacitor is Pav = {tex}\frac{W}{T}=\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} d W=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 \omega t}{2 \omega}\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}\left[\cos \frac{4 \pi}{T} t\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[1-1]=0{/tex} Thus the average power dissipated per cycle in a capacitor is zero.
solution_html:
Average power associated with a capacitor: When an a.c. is applied to a capacitor, the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} radian. So we write the expressions for instantaneous voltage and current as follows:
{tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex}
and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex}
Work done in the circuit in small time dt will be
dW = Pdt = VIdt = V0I0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t dt
{tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex}
The average power dissipated per cycle in the capacitor is
Pav = {tex}\frac{W}{T}=\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} d W=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 \omega t}{2 \omega}\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}\left[\cos \frac{4 \pi}{T} t\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[1-1]=0{/tex} 
Thus the average power dissipated per cycle in a capacitor is zero.

question_html:
1. Draw a schematic arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coils in a transformer when the two coils are wound on top of each other.
  2. State the underlying principle of a step-up transformer and obtain the expression for the ratio of secondary to primary voltage in terms of the :
    1. number of secondary and primary windings and
    2. primary and secondary currents.
  3. Write the main assumption involved in deriving the above relations.
  4. How is the transformer used in large scale transmission and distribution of electrical energy over long distances?
question_text: Draw a schematic arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coils in a transformer when the two coils are wound on top of each other. State the underlying principle of a step-up transformer and obtain the expression for the ratio of secondary to primary voltage in terms of the : number of secondary and primary windings and primary and secondary currents. Write the main assumption involved in deriving the above relations. How is the transformer used in large scale transmission and distribution of electrical energy over long distances?
explanation: Principle of a transformer : When an alternating current flows through the primary coil, an emf is induced in the neighbouring (secondary) coil. Let {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} be the rate of change of flux through each turn of the primary and the secondary coil {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2}=-N_1 \frac{d \phi}{d t} /-N_2 \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}{/tex} ...(i) But for an idea transformer, V 1 I 1 = V 2 I 2 {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{I_2}{I_1}{/tex} ..(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}=\frac{I_2}{I_1}{/tex} Main assumptions (i) The primary resistance and current are small. (ii) The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is same / there is no leakage of flux from the core. (iii) Secondary current is small. Reason due to which energy loses may occur : Flux resistance/Resistance of the coils/ Eddy currents/ Hysteresis
solution: Principle of a transformer : When an alternating current flows through the primary coil, an emf is induced in the neighbouring (secondary) coil. Let {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} be the rate of change of flux through each turn of the primary and the secondary coil {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2}=-N_1 \frac{d \phi}{d t} /-N_2 \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}{/tex} ...(i) But for an idea transformer, V 1 I 1 = V 2 I 2 {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{I_2}{I_1}{/tex} ..(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}=\frac{I_2}{I_1}{/tex} Main assumptions (i) The primary resistance and current are small. (ii) The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is same / there is no leakage of flux from the core. (iii) Secondary current is small. Reason due to which energy loses may occur : Flux resistance/Resistance of the coils/ Eddy currents/ Hysteresis
solution_html:
1. Principle of a transformer : When an alternating current flows through the primary coil, an emf is induced in the neighbouring (secondary) coil.
    1. Let {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} be the rate of change of flux through each turn of the primary and the secondary coil
      {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_2}=-N_1 \frac{d \phi}{d t} /-N_2 \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}{/tex}
      or {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}{/tex} ...(i)
    2. But for an idea transformer, V1I1 = V2I2
      {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{I_2}{I_1}{/tex} ..(ii)
      From (i) and (ii)
      {tex}\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{N_1}{N_2}=\frac{I_2}{I_1}{/tex}
  3. Main assumptions (i) The primary resistance and current are small. (ii) The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is same / there is no leakage of flux from the core. (iii) Secondary current is small.
  4. Reason due to which energy loses may occur : Flux resistance/Resistance of the coils/ Eddy currents/ Hysteresis

question_html: A sinusoidal emf is applied to a circuit containing an inductor only. Show that the current lags behind the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}. Show this phase relationship graphically.
question_text: A sinusoidal emf is applied to a circuit containing an inductor only. Show that the current lags behind the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Show this phase relationship graphically.
explanation: The instantaneous value of alternating voltage applied V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t ...(i) If i is the instantaneous current in the circuit and di/dt, the rate of change of current in the circuit at that instant, then instantaneous induced emf {tex}\varepsilon=-L \frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} According to Kirchhoff's second law in closed circuit. or V + {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V - L {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = 0 V = L {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{L}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_0 \sin \omega t}{L}{/tex} or di = {tex}\frac{V_0 \sin \omega t}{L}{/tex} dt i = {tex}\frac{V_0}{L} \int \sin \omega{/tex} tdt = {tex}\frac{V_0}{L}\left\{-\frac{\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{V_0}{\omega L}{/tex} cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t = {tex}-\frac{V_0}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} or i = {tex}\frac{V_0}{\omega L} \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ...(ii) This is required expression for current or i = i 0 sin {tex}\left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ...(iii) Graphically
solution: The instantaneous value of alternating voltage applied V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t ...(i) If i is the instantaneous current in the circuit and di/dt, the rate of change of current in the circuit at that instant, then instantaneous induced emf {tex}\varepsilon=-L \frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} According to Kirchhoff's second law in closed circuit. or V + {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V - L {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = 0 V = L {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{L}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_0 \sin \omega t}{L}{/tex} or di = {tex}\frac{V_0 \sin \omega t}{L}{/tex} dt i = {tex}\frac{V_0}{L} \int \sin \omega{/tex} tdt = {tex}\frac{V_0}{L}\left\{-\frac{\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{V_0}{\omega L}{/tex} cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t = {tex}-\frac{V_0}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} or i = {tex}\frac{V_0}{\omega L} \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ...(ii) This is required expression for current or i = i 0 sin {tex}\left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ...(iii) Graphically
solution_html:
The instantaneous value of alternating voltage applied V = V0sin{tex}\omega{/tex}t ...(i)
If i is the instantaneous current in the circuit and di/dt, the rate of change of current in the circuit at that instant, then instantaneous induced emf
{tex}\varepsilon=-L \frac{d i}{d t}{/tex}
According to Kirchhoff's second law in closed circuit. or
V + {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V - L{tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = 0
V = L{tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{L}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d i}{d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_0 \sin \omega t}{L}{/tex} or di = {tex}\frac{V_0 \sin \omega t}{L}{/tex}dt
i = {tex}\frac{V_0}{L} \int \sin \omega{/tex}tdt = {tex}\frac{V_0}{L}\left\{-\frac{\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{V_0}{\omega L}{/tex}cos{tex}\omega{/tex}t
= {tex}-\frac{V_0}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex}
or i = {tex}\frac{V_0}{\omega L} \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ...(ii)
This is required expression for current or
i = i0 sin {tex}\left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
Graphically

question_html: A power transmission line feeds input power at 2200 V to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having 3000 turns. Find the number of turns in the secondary winding to get the power output at 220 V.
question_text: A power transmission line feeds input power at 2200 V to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having 3000 turns. Find the number of turns in the secondary winding to get the power output at 220 V.
explanation: Given, input voltage (V 1 ) = 2200 V Number of turns (N 1 ) = 3000 Output voltage (V 2 ) = 220 V As, {tex}\frac { V _ { 2 } } { V _ { 1 } } = \frac { N _ { 2 } } { N _ { 1 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 220 } { 2200 } = \frac { N _ { 2 } } { 3000 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad N _ { 2 } = \frac { 220 } { 2200 } \times 3000{/tex} = 300 turns
solution: Given, input voltage (V 1 ) = 2200 V Number of turns (N 1 ) = 3000 Output voltage (V 2 ) = 220 V As, {tex}\frac { V _ { 2 } } { V _ { 1 } } = \frac { N _ { 2 } } { N _ { 1 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 220 } { 2200 } = \frac { N _ { 2 } } { 3000 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad N _ { 2 } = \frac { 220 } { 2200 } \times 3000{/tex} = 300 turns
solution_html:
Given, input voltage (V1) = 2200 V
Number of turns (N1) = 3000
Output voltage (V2) = 220 V
As, {tex}\frac { V _ { 2 } } { V _ { 1 } } = \frac { N _ { 2 } } { N _ { 1 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 220 } { 2200 } = \frac { N _ { 2 } } { 3000 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad N _ { 2 } = \frac { 220 } { 2200 } \times 3000{/tex} = 300 turns

question_html: An ac voltage V = V0sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied to a pure inductor L. Obtain an expression for the current in the circuit. Prove that the average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is zero.
question_text: An ac voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied to a pure inductor L. Obtain an expression for the current in the circuit. Prove that the average power supplied to an inductor over one complete cycle is zero.
explanation: If an alternating voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied across pure inductor of inductance L, then the magnitude of induced emf will be equal to the applied voltage, {tex}E=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} For the circuit, the magnitude of induced emf = applied voltage {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}d I=\frac{V_{0}}{L}{/tex} sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} dt Integrating both the sides, we get {tex}I=\frac{V_{0}}{L} \int \sin \omega t d t=\frac{V_{0}}{L}\left(\frac{-\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right){/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \cos \omega t=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} sin ( {tex}-\theta{/tex} )= - {tex}\theta{/tex} hence the equation is modified to {tex}I=\frac{V_{0}}{X_{L}} \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} thus the current in the circuit ,I = I 0 sin {tex}\left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} this is the required expression. The average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is P av = E rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} When the circuit carries an ideal inductor, then the phase difference between the current and voltage is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} In case of the pure inductive circuit, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} But cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 0 So power dissipated = 0 Hence, when an ac source is connected to an ideal inductor, the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
solution: If an alternating voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied across pure inductor of inductance L, then the magnitude of induced emf will be equal to the applied voltage, {tex}E=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} For the circuit, the magnitude of induced emf = applied voltage {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}d I=\frac{V_{0}}{L}{/tex} sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} dt Integrating both the sides, we get {tex}I=\frac{V_{0}}{L} \int \sin \omega t d t=\frac{V_{0}}{L}\left(\frac{-\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right){/tex} {tex}I=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \cos \omega t=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} sin ( {tex}-\theta{/tex} )= - {tex}\theta{/tex} hence the equation is modified to {tex}I=\frac{V_{0}}{X_{L}} \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} thus the current in the circuit ,I = I 0 sin {tex}\left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} this is the required expression. The average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is P av = E rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} When the circuit carries an ideal inductor, then the phase difference between the current and voltage is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} In case of the pure inductive circuit, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} But cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 0 So power dissipated = 0 Hence, when an ac source is connected to an ideal inductor, the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
solution_html:
1. If an alternating voltage V = V0sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied across pure inductor of inductance L, then the magnitude of induced emf will be equal to the applied voltage,
    {tex}E=L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
    For the circuit, the magnitude of induced emf = applied voltage
    {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}
    {tex}d I=\frac{V_{0}}{L}{/tex} sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} dt
    Integrating both the sides, we get
    {tex}I=\frac{V_{0}}{L} \int \sin \omega t d t=\frac{V_{0}}{L}\left(\frac{-\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right){/tex}
    {tex}I=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \cos \omega t=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} sin ({tex}-\theta{/tex})= -{tex}\theta{/tex} hence the equation is modified to 
    {tex}I=\frac{V_{0}}{X_{L}} \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} 
    thus the current in the circuit ,I = I0 sin {tex}\left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}  this is the required expression.
  2. The average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is
    Pav = Erms Irms cos {tex}\phi{/tex}
    When the circuit carries an ideal inductor, then the phase difference between the current and voltage is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    In case of the pure inductive circuit,
    {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    But cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 0
    So power dissipated = 0
    Hence, when an ac source is connected to an ideal inductor, the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.

question_html: A source of ac voltage V = V0sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}, is connected across a pure inductor of inductance L. Derive the expressions for the instantaneous current in the circuit. Show that average power dissipated in the circuit is zero.
question_text: A source of ac voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} , is connected across a pure inductor of inductance L. Derive the expressions for the instantaneous current in the circuit. Show that average power dissipated in the circuit is zero.
explanation: Given: V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} V = {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow d i=\frac{V}{L} d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore d i=\frac{V_{0}}{L} \sin \omega t d t{/tex} Integrating, i = {tex}-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore i=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right)=I_{0} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} where, I 0 = {tex}\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L}{/tex} Average power, {tex}P_{a v}=\int_\limits{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-V_{0}^{2}}{\omega L} \int_{0}^{T}{/tex} sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} dt {tex}=\frac{-V_{0}^{2}}{2 \omega L} \int_\limits{0}^{T}{/tex} sin (2 {tex}\omega t{/tex} ) dt = 0 thus the average power dissipated in the circuit is zero
solution: Given: V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} V = {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow d i=\frac{V}{L} d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore d i=\frac{V_{0}}{L} \sin \omega t d t{/tex} Integrating, i = {tex}-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore i=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right)=I_{0} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex} where, I 0 = {tex}\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L}{/tex} Average power, {tex}P_{a v}=\int_\limits{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-V_{0}^{2}}{\omega L} \int_{0}^{T}{/tex} sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} dt {tex}=\frac{-V_{0}^{2}}{2 \omega L} \int_\limits{0}^{T}{/tex} sin (2 {tex}\omega t{/tex} ) dt = 0 thus the average power dissipated in the circuit is zero
solution_html:
Given: V = V0sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}
V = {tex}L \frac{d i}{d t} \Rightarrow d i=\frac{V}{L} d t{/tex}

{tex}\therefore d i=\frac{V_{0}}{L} \sin \omega t d t{/tex}
Integrating, i = {tex}-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \cos \omega t{/tex}
{tex}\therefore i=-\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right)=I_{0} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\omega t\right){/tex}
where, I0 = {tex}\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L}{/tex}
Average power,
{tex}P_{a v}=\int_\limits{0}^{T} V i d t{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-V_{0}^{2}}{\omega L} \int_{0}^{T}{/tex} sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} cos {tex}\omega t{/tex} dt
{tex}=\frac{-V_{0}^{2}}{2 \omega L} \int_\limits{0}^{T}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\omega t{/tex}) dt
= 0 thus the average power dissipated in the circuit is zero

question_html: A power transmission line feeds input power at 2300 V to a step down transformer with its primary windings having 4000 turns. What should be the number of turns in the secondary in order to get output power at 230 V?
question_text: A power transmission line feeds input power at 2300 V to a step down transformer with its primary windings having 4000 turns. What should be the number of turns in the secondary in order to get output power at 230 V?
solution_html:
V1 = 2300 V, N1 = 4000, V2 = 230 V
Now, {tex}\frac{{{V_2}}}{{{V_1}}} = \frac{{{N_2}}}{{{N_1}}}{/tex}
or {tex}{N_2} = {N_1}\frac{{{V_2}}}{{{V_1}}} = 4000 \times \frac{{230}}{{2300}}{/tex} = 400 turns

question_html:
In the following circuit, calculate:

  1. the capacitance of the capacitor, if the power factor of the circuit is unity,
  2. the Q-factor of this circuit. What is the significance of the Q-factor in ac circuit? Given the angular frequency of the ac source to be 100 rad/s. Calculate the average power dissipated in the circuit.
question_text: In the following circuit, calculate: the capacitance of the capacitor, if the power factor of the circuit is unity, the Q-factor of this circuit. What is the significance of the Q-factor in ac circuit? Given the angular frequency of the ac source to be 100 rad/s. Calculate the average power dissipated in the circuit.
explanation: Calculation of Capacitance As power factor is unity, {tex}\therefore{/tex} X L = X C also L=200mH and R= 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex} 100 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{200 \times 10^{-3} \times C}}{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} C = 1 hence ca[pacitance is given by ,C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{3}}{/tex} F = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 F = 0.5mF Q-factor of circuit and its importance Calculation of average power dissipated Quality factor, {tex}Q=\frac{1}{R} \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \times{/tex} 20 = 2 Significance: It measures the sharpness of resnance. Average Power dissipated, P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}=50 \times \frac{50}{10} \times{/tex} 1 W = 250 watts
solution: Calculation of Capacitance As power factor is unity, {tex}\therefore{/tex} X L = X C also L=200mH and R= 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex} 100 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{200 \times 10^{-3} \times C}}{/tex} 10 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} C = 1 hence ca[pacitance is given by ,C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{3}}{/tex} F = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 F = 0.5mF Q-factor of circuit and its importance Calculation of average power dissipated Quality factor, {tex}Q=\frac{1}{R} \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \times{/tex} 20 = 2 Significance: It measures the sharpness of resnance. Average Power dissipated, P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}=50 \times \frac{50}{10} \times{/tex} 1 W = 250 watts
solution_html:
1. Calculation of Capacitance
    As power factor is unity,
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} XL = XC  also L=200mH and R= 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex}
    100 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{200 \times 10^{-3} \times C}}{/tex}
    104 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} C = 1
    hence ca[pacitance is given by ,C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{3}}{/tex} F
    = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 F
    = 0.5mF
  2. Q-factor of circuit and its importance Calculation of average power dissipated
    Quality factor, {tex}Q=\frac{1}{R} \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \times{/tex} 20 = 2
    Significance: It measures the sharpness of resnance.
    Average Power dissipated, 
    P = Vrms Irms cos {tex}\phi{/tex}
    {tex}=50 \times \frac{50}{10} \times{/tex} 1 W
    = 250 watts

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Describe, with the help of a suitable diagram, the working principle of a step-up transformer. Obtain the relation between input and output voltages in terms of the number of turns of primary and secondary windings and the currents in the input and output circuits.
  2. Given the input current 15 A and the input voltage of 100 V for a step-up transformer having 90% efficiency, find the output power and the voltage in the secondary if the output current is 3 A.
question_text: Describe, with the help of a suitable diagram, the working principle of a step-up transformer. Obtain the relation between input and output voltages in terms of the number of turns of primary and secondary windings and the currents in the input and output circuits. Given the input current 15 A and the input voltage of 100 V for a step-up transformer having 90% efficiency, find the output power and the voltage in the secondary if the output current is 3 A.
explanation: Working principle: Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighboring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input - Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Input power, P i = I i {tex}\times{/tex} V i = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1500 W Power output, P 0 = P i {tex}\times \frac{90}{100}{/tex} = 1350 W {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 0 V = 1350 W Output voltage, V 0 = {tex}\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} V = 450 V
solution: Working principle: Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighboring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input - Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Input power, P i = I i {tex}\times{/tex} V i = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1500 W Power output, P 0 = P i {tex}\times \frac{90}{100}{/tex} = 1350 W {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 0 V = 1350 W Output voltage, V 0 = {tex}\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} V = 450 V
solution_html:
1. Working principle:
    Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighboring coil (Principle of mutual induction)
    Voltage across secondary
    Vs = es = -Ns {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    Voltage across primary
    Vp = ep = -Np {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, Ns > Np)
    In an ideal transformer
    Power Input - Power output
    Ip Vp = Is Vs
    {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex}


  2. Input power, Pi = Ii {tex}\times{/tex} Vi = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1500 W
    Power output, P0 = Pi {tex}\times \frac{90}{100}{/tex} = 1350 W
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I0 V = 1350 W
    Output voltage, V0 = {tex}\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} V = 450 V

question_html:
1. Draw a labelled diagram of a step-up transformer. Obtain the ratio of secondary to primary voltage in terms of the number of turns and currents in the two coils.
  2. A power transmission line feeds input power at 2200 V to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having 3000 turns. Find the number of turns in the secondary to get the power output at 220 V.
question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of a step-up transformer. Obtain the ratio of secondary to primary voltage in terms of the number of turns and currents in the two coils. A power transmission line feeds input power at 2200 V to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having 3000 turns. Find the number of turns in the secondary to get the power output at 220 V.
explanation: Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighbouring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input = Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Calculation of the number of turns in the secondary: No. of turns in primary coil N p = 3000, V s = 220V, V p = 2200V {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{3000}=\frac{220}{2200}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} N s = 300
solution: Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighbouring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input = Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Calculation of the number of turns in the secondary: No. of turns in primary coil N p = 3000, V s = 220V, V p = 2200V {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{3000}=\frac{220}{2200}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} N s = 300
solution_html:
1. Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighbouring coil (Principle of mutual induction)
    Voltage across secondary
    Vs = es = -Ns {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    Voltage across primary
    Vp = ep = -Np {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, Ns > Np)
    In an ideal transformer
    Power Input = Power output
    Ip Vp = Is Vs
    {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex}
  2. Calculation of the number of turns in the secondary:
    No. of turns in primary coil Np = 3000, Vs = 220V, Vp = 2200V
    {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{3000}=\frac{220}{2200}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Ns = 300

question_html: Given the input current 15 A and the input voltage of 100 V for a step-up transformer having 90% efficiency, What will be the output power and the voltage in the secondary if the output current is 3 A.
question_text: Given the input current 15 A and the input voltage of 100 V for a step-up transformer having 90% efficiency, What will be the output power and the voltage in the secondary if the output current is 3 A.
explanation: Input current = 15A Input voltage = 100 V efficiency = 90 {tex}\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Input power }}=0.9{/tex} {tex} \frac{\text { Output power }}{{ Input \;voltage\; \times Input \;current }}=\frac{\text { Output power }}{15 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.9 Output power = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.9 = 1350 W Output current = 3A Output voltage = {tex}\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Output current }}=\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} = 450 V
solution: Input current = 15A Input voltage = 100 V efficiency = 90 {tex}\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Input power }}=0.9{/tex} {tex} \frac{\text { Output power }}{{ Input \;voltage\; \times Input \;current }}=\frac{\text { Output power }}{15 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.9 Output power = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.9 = 1350 W Output current = 3A Output voltage = {tex}\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Output current }}=\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} = 450 V
solution_html:
Input current = 15A
Input voltage = 100 V
efficiency = 90
{tex}\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Input power }}=0.9{/tex}
{tex} \frac{\text { Output power }}{{ Input \;voltage\; \times Input \;current }}=\frac{\text { Output power }}{15 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.9
Output power = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.9 = 1350 W
Output current = 3A
Output voltage = {tex}\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Output current }}=\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} = 450 V

question_html:
The primary coil of an ideal step-up trans-former has 100 turns and the transformation ratio is also 100. The input voltage and the power are 220 V and 1100 W respectively. Calculate:

  1. number of turns in the secondary
  2. the current in the primary
  3. voltage across the secondary
  4. the current in the secondary
  5. power in the secondary.
question_text: The primary coil of an ideal step-up trans-former has 100 turns and the transformation ratio is also 100. The input voltage and the power are 220 V and 1100 W respectively. Calculate: number of turns in the secondary the current in the primary voltage across the secondary the current in the secondary power in the secondary.
explanation: Here N 1 = 100, {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=220 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} , P 1 = 1100 W Transformation ratio, {tex}k=\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}=100{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} N 2 = 100 N = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 10,000 {tex}I_{1}=\frac{P_{1}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{1100}{220}=5 {A}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{2}=k \varepsilon_{1}=100 \times 220{/tex} = 22,000 V {tex}I_{2}=\frac{I_{1}}{k}=\frac{5}{100}=0.05 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} For an ideal transformer Output power = Input power = 1100 W
solution: Here N 1 = 100, {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=220 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} , P 1 = 1100 W Transformation ratio, {tex}k=\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}=100{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} N 2 = 100 N = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 10,000 {tex}I_{1}=\frac{P_{1}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{1100}{220}=5 {A}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{2}=k \varepsilon_{1}=100 \times 220{/tex} = 22,000 V {tex}I_{2}=\frac{I_{1}}{k}=\frac{5}{100}=0.05 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} For an ideal transformer Output power = Input power = 1100 W
solution_html:
Here N1 = 100, {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=220 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}, P1 = 1100 W

  1. Transformation ratio, {tex}k=\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}=100{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} N2 = 100 N = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 10,000
  2. {tex}I_{1}=\frac{P_{1}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{1100}{220}=5 {A}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\varepsilon_{2}=k \varepsilon_{1}=100 \times 220{/tex} = 22,000 V
  4. {tex}I_{2}=\frac{I_{1}}{k}=\frac{5}{100}=0.05 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
  5. For an ideal transformer
    Output power = Input power = 1100 W

question_html:
1. Draw an arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coils in a transformer with two coils on a separate limb of the core. State the underlying principle of a transformer. Deduce the expression for the ratio of secondary voltage to the primary voltage in terms of the ratio of the number of turns of primary and secondary winding. For an ideal transformer, obtain the ratio of primary and secondary currents in terms of the ratio of the voltages in the secondary and primary voltages.
  2. Write any two reasons for the energy losses which occur in actual transformers.
  3. A step-up transformer converts a low input voltage into a high output voltage. Does it violate law of conservation of energy? Explain.
question_text: Draw an arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coils in a transformer with two coils on a separate limb of the core. State the underlying principle of a transformer. Deduce the expression for the ratio of secondary voltage to the primary voltage in terms of the ratio of the number of turns of primary and secondary winding. For an ideal transformer, obtain the ratio of primary and secondary currents in terms of the ratio of the voltages in the secondary and primary voltages. Write any two reasons for the energy losses which occur in actual transformers. A step-up transformer converts a low input voltage into a high output voltage. Does it violate law of conservation of energy? Explain.
explanation: Transformer work on the principle of mutual induction. Whenever the current in the primary coil changes an emf is induced in the secondary coil. E p = {tex}\frac{-N_P d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ...(1) where {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} is change in flux. N P is the number of turns in the primary & E P is the primary voltage. Secondary voltage E s = {tex}\frac{-N_s d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ...(2) where N s is number of turns of secondary coil from (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{E_P}{E_S}=\frac{N_P}{N_S}{/tex} ...(3) For an ideal transformer input power = output power E P i P = E s i s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_P}{E_S}=\frac{i_S}{i_P}{/tex} ...(4) From (3) and (4) {tex}\frac{i_S}{i_P}=\frac{N_P}{N_S}{/tex} Energy losses in transformer: Eddy current loss Eddy current in iron core of transformer facilitates the loss of energy in the form of heat. Flux leakage Total fluxes linked with primary do not completely pass through the secondary which denotes the loss in the flux. The law of conservation of energy cannot be violated at all. The fact that voltage and current get “stepped” in opposite directions makes perfect sense when power is equal to voltage times current, and realize that transformers cannot produce power, only convert it. Any device that could output more power than it took in would violate the Law of Energy Conservation in physics, namely that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted. It makes a difference in number of turns. In a step up transformer, the primary has lower number of turns than the secondary and vice versa in the case of step down transformer.
solution: Transformer work on the principle of mutual induction. Whenever the current in the primary coil changes an emf is induced in the secondary coil. E p = {tex}\frac{-N_P d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ...(1) where {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} is change in flux. N P is the number of turns in the primary & E P is the primary voltage. Secondary voltage E s = {tex}\frac{-N_s d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ...(2) where N s is number of turns of secondary coil from (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{E_P}{E_S}=\frac{N_P}{N_S}{/tex} ...(3) For an ideal transformer input power = output power E P i P = E s i s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_P}{E_S}=\frac{i_S}{i_P}{/tex} ...(4) From (3) and (4) {tex}\frac{i_S}{i_P}=\frac{N_P}{N_S}{/tex} Energy losses in transformer: Eddy current loss Eddy current in iron core of transformer facilitates the loss of energy in the form of heat. Flux leakage Total fluxes linked with primary do not completely pass through the secondary which denotes the loss in the flux. The law of conservation of energy cannot be violated at all. The fact that voltage and current get “stepped” in opposite directions makes perfect sense when power is equal to voltage times current, and realize that transformers cannot produce power, only convert it. Any device that could output more power than it took in would violate the Law of Energy Conservation in physics, namely that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted. It makes a difference in number of turns. In a step up transformer, the primary has lower number of turns than the secondary and vice versa in the case of step down transformer.
solution_html:
1. Transformer work on the principle of mutual induction. Whenever the current in the primary coil changes an emf is induced in the secondary coil.
    Ep = {tex}\frac{-N_P d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ...(1)
    where {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} is change in flux.
    NP is the number of turns in the primary & EP is the primary voltage.
    Secondary voltage
    Es = {tex}\frac{-N_s d \phi}{d t}{/tex} ...(2) where Ns is number of turns of secondary coil from (1) and (2)
    {tex}\frac{E_P}{E_S}=\frac{N_P}{N_S}{/tex} ...(3)
    For an ideal transformer input power = output power
    EPiP = Esis {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_P}{E_S}=\frac{i_S}{i_P}{/tex} ...(4)
    From (3) and (4)
    {tex}\frac{i_S}{i_P}=\frac{N_P}{N_S}{/tex}

  2. Energy losses in transformer:

    • Eddy current loss Eddy current in iron core of transformer facilitates the loss of energy in the form of heat.
    • Flux leakage Total fluxes linked with primary do not completely pass through the secondary which denotes the loss in the flux.

  3. The law of conservation of energy cannot be violated at all. The fact that voltage and current get “stepped” in opposite directions makes perfect sense when power is equal to voltage times current, and realize that transformers cannot produce power, only convert it. Any device that could output more power than it took in would violate the Law of Energy Conservation in physics, namely that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted.
    It makes a difference in number of turns. In a step up transformer, the primary has lower number of turns than the secondary and vice versa in the case of step down transformer.

question_html: Obtain the expression for the energy density of magnetic field B produced in the inductor.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the energy density of magnetic field B produced in the inductor.
explanation: Instantaneous Induced emf in an inductor when current changes through it {tex}e=-L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Hence, instantaneous applied voltage e = V = {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Work done, dW = V.dq = Vldt {tex}\therefore{/tex} dW = LIdI {tex}\Rightarrow \int d W=\int_{0}^{l} L I d I{/tex} energy in inductor is given by: W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} Energy density, {tex}u=\frac{\text { total energy stored }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} ,volume=A {tex}l{/tex} {tex}u=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) L l^{2}}{A l}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}(L I) I}{A l}{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = NBA = LI and {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \Rightarrow I=\frac{B l}{\mu_{0} N}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U=\frac{\frac{1}{2}(N B A) \cdot \frac{B l}{\mu_{0} N}}{A l}=\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex}
solution: Instantaneous Induced emf in an inductor when current changes through it {tex}e=-L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Hence, instantaneous applied voltage e = V = {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} Work done, dW = V.dq = Vldt {tex}\therefore{/tex} dW = LIdI {tex}\Rightarrow \int d W=\int_{0}^{l} L I d I{/tex} energy in inductor is given by: W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex} Energy density, {tex}u=\frac{\text { total energy stored }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} ,volume=A {tex}l{/tex} {tex}u=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) L l^{2}}{A l}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}(L I) I}{A l}{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} = NBA = LI and {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \Rightarrow I=\frac{B l}{\mu_{0} N}{/tex} {tex}\therefore U=\frac{\frac{1}{2}(N B A) \cdot \frac{B l}{\mu_{0} N}}{A l}=\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Instantaneous Induced emf in an inductor when current changes through it
{tex}e=-L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
Hence, instantaneous applied voltage
e = V = {tex}L \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
Work done, dW = V.dq = Vldt
{tex}\therefore{/tex} dW = LIdI
{tex}\Rightarrow \int d W=\int_{0}^{l} L I d I{/tex}
energy in inductor is given by: W = {tex}\frac{1}{2} L I^{2}{/tex}
Energy density, {tex}u=\frac{\text { total energy stored }}{\text { volume }}{/tex} ,volume=A{tex}l{/tex}
{tex}u=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) L l^{2}}{A l}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}(L I) I}{A l}{/tex}
{tex}\phi{/tex} = NBA = LI
and {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{l} \Rightarrow I=\frac{B l}{\mu_{0} N}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore U=\frac{\frac{1}{2}(N B A) \cdot \frac{B l}{\mu_{0} N}}{A l}=\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}{/tex}

question_html: A 200 mH (pure) inductor and a 5 {tex}\mu{/tex}F (pure) capacitor are connected one by one, across a sinusoidal ac voltage source of V = [70.7sin (1000 t)] voltage. Obtain the expression for the current in each case.
question_text: A 200 mH (pure) inductor and a 5 {tex}\mu{/tex} F (pure) capacitor are connected one by one, across a sinusoidal ac voltage source of V = [70.7sin (1000 t)] voltage. Obtain the expression for the current in each case.
explanation: For the applied voltage L=200X10 -3 H. V = 70.7 sin (1000 t), we have V 0 = 70.7 volts {tex}\omega{/tex} = 1000 rad/s For the inductor {tex}i_{0}=\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L}=\frac{70.7}{1000 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{A}{/tex} = 35.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 A = 0.3535 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Expression for current in in case of inductor is given by:- i = (0.3535)sin {tex}\left(1000 t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} For the capacitor {tex}i_{d F}=\frac{V_{0}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)}=V_{0} \cdot \omega C{/tex} = 70.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 A = 353.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A = 0.3535 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Expression for current in case of conductor is given by:- I = 0.3535 sin {tex}\left(1000 t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
solution: For the applied voltage L=200X10 -3 H. V = 70.7 sin (1000 t), we have V 0 = 70.7 volts {tex}\omega{/tex} = 1000 rad/s For the inductor {tex}i_{0}=\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L}=\frac{70.7}{1000 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{A}{/tex} = 35.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 A = 0.3535 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Expression for current in in case of inductor is given by:- i = (0.3535)sin {tex}\left(1000 t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} For the capacitor {tex}i_{d F}=\frac{V_{0}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)}=V_{0} \cdot \omega C{/tex} = 70.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 A = 353.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 A = 0.3535 A {tex}\therefore{/tex} Expression for current in case of conductor is given by:- I = 0.3535 sin {tex}\left(1000 t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
For the applied voltage L=200X10-3 H.
V = 70.7 sin (1000 t), we have
V0 = 70.7 volts
{tex}\omega{/tex} = 1000 rad/s
For the inductor
{tex}i_{0}=\frac{V_{0}}{\omega L}=\frac{70.7}{1000 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{A}{/tex}
= 35.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 A
= 0.3535 A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Expression for current in in case of inductor is given by:-
i = (0.3535)sin {tex}\left(1000 t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
For the capacitor
{tex}i_{d F}=\frac{V_{0}}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)}=V_{0} \cdot \omega C{/tex}
= 70.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 A
= 353.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 A = 0.3535 A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Expression for current in case of conductor is given by:-
I = 0.3535 sin {tex}\left(1000 t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Write the function of a transformer. State its principle of working with the help of a diagram. Mention various energy losses in this device.
question_text: Write the function of a transformer. State its principle of working with the help of a diagram. Mention various energy losses in this device.
explanation: A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil. Energy losses in the transformer: Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat. Hysteresis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat.
solution: A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil. Energy losses in the transformer: Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat. Hysteresis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat.
solution_html:
A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil.

Energy losses in the transformer:

  1. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core.
  2. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings.
  3. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat.
  4. Hysteresis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat.

question_html:
1. When an AC source is connected to an ideal inductor show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
  2. A lamp is connected in series with an inductor and an AC source. What happens to the brightness of the lamp when the key is plugged in and an iron rod is inserted inside the inductor? Explain.
question_text: When an AC source is connected to an ideal inductor show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero. A lamp is connected in series with an inductor and an AC source. What happens to the brightness of the lamp when the key is plugged in and an iron rod is inserted inside the inductor? Explain.
explanation: As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex} In an ideal inductor, rms current I rms lags behind the applied rms voltage V rms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex} or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex} The brightness of the lamp decreases. It is because when iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, its inductance L increases(as inductance depends directly on the permeability of the material inside it i.e., {tex}L\propto\mu{/tex} , thereby increasing its inductive reactance X L as permeability of iron rod is greater than that of free space or air and hence impedance Z of the circuit. As I rms = V rms /Z, so, this decreases the current I rms in the circuit and hence decreases the brightness of lamp.
solution: As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex} In an ideal inductor, rms current I rms lags behind the applied rms voltage V rms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex} or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex} The brightness of the lamp decreases. It is because when iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, its inductance L increases(as inductance depends directly on the permeability of the material inside it i.e., {tex}L\propto\mu{/tex} , thereby increasing its inductive reactance X L as permeability of iron rod is greater than that of free space or air and hence impedance Z of the circuit. As I rms = V rms /Z, so, this decreases the current I rms in the circuit and hence decreases the brightness of lamp.
solution_html:
1. As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex}
    In an ideal inductor, rms current  Irms lags behind the applied rms voltage Vrms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex}
    or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex}
  2. The brightness of the lamp decreases. It is because when iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, its inductance L increases(as inductance depends directly on the permeability of the material inside it i.e., {tex}L\propto\mu{/tex}, thereby increasing its inductive reactance XL as permeability of iron rod is greater than that of free space or air and hence impedance Z of the circuit. As Irms = Vrms/Z, so, this decreases the current Irms in the circuit and hence decreases the brightness of lamp.

question_text: Write the function of a transformer. State its principle of working with the help of a diagram. Mention various energy losses in this device. The primary coil of an ideal step-up transformer has 100 turns and transformation ratio is also 100. The input voltage and power are respectively 220 V and 1100 W. Calculate number of turns in secondary current in primary voltage across secondary current in secondary power in secondary
explanation: A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil. Energy losses in the transformer: a. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. b. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings. c. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat. d. Hysterisis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat. Now, N = {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{S}}{100}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N S = 10000 turns Current in primary is given by, I P V P = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I P = {tex}\frac{1100}{220}{/tex} = 5A Voltage across secondary is given by, {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} = N {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V S = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 22000 V Current in secondary is given by V S I S = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I S = {tex}\frac{P}{V_{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1100}{22000}{/tex} = 0.05 A In an ideal transformer, Power in secondary = Power in primary = 1100 W
solution: A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil. Energy losses in the transformer: a. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. b. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings. c. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat. d. Hysterisis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat. Now, N = {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{S}}{100}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N S = 10000 turns Current in primary is given by, I P V P = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I P = {tex}\frac{1100}{220}{/tex} = 5A Voltage across secondary is given by, {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} = N {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V S = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 22000 V Current in secondary is given by V S I S = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I S = {tex}\frac{P}{V_{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1100}{22000}{/tex} = 0.05 A In an ideal transformer, Power in secondary = Power in primary = 1100 W
solution_html:
1. A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil.

    Energy losses in the transformer:
    a. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. 
    b. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings.
    c. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat.
    d. Hysterisis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat.
    1. Now,
      N = {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{S}}{100}{/tex} = 100
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} NS = 10000 turns
    2. Current in primary is given by,
      IPVP = P
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IP = {tex}\frac{1100}{220}{/tex} = 5A
    3. Voltage across secondary is given by,
      {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} = N
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VS = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 22000 V
    4. Current in secondary is given by
      VSIS = P
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IS = {tex}\frac{P}{V_{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1100}{22000}{/tex} = 0.05 A
    5. In an ideal transformer, 
      Power in secondary = Power in primary = 1100 W

question_html:
1. Draw a labelled diagram of a step-down transformer. State the principle of its working.
  2. Express the turn ratio in terms of voltages.
  3. Find the ratio of primary and secondary currents in terms of turn ratio in an ideal transformer.
  4. How much current is drawn by the primary of a transformer connected to 220 V supply when it delivers power to a 110 V - 550 W refrigerator?
question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of a step-down transformer. State the principle of its working. Express the turn ratio in terms of voltages. Find the ratio of primary and secondary currents in terms of turn ratio in an ideal transformer. How much current is drawn by the primary of a transformer connected to 220 V supply when it delivers power to a 110 V - 550 W refrigerator?
explanation: Principle: Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. When the current flowing through the primary coil changes, an emf is induced in the secondary coil due to the change in magnetic flux linked with it. {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} For an ideal transformer, i p V p = i s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{i_{p}}{i_{s}}=\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} We have i p V p = i s V s = 550 W V p = 220 V i p = {tex}\frac{550}{220}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 A
solution: Principle: Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. When the current flowing through the primary coil changes, an emf is induced in the secondary coil due to the change in magnetic flux linked with it. {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} For an ideal transformer, i p V p = i s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{i_{p}}{i_{s}}=\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} We have i p V p = i s V s = 550 W V p = 220 V i p = {tex}\frac{550}{220}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 A
solution_html:
1. Principle: Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. When the current flowing through the primary coil changes, an emf is induced in the secondary coil due to the change in magnetic flux linked with it.
  2. {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex}
  3. For an ideal transformer,
    ip Vp = is Vs
    {tex}\therefore \frac{i_{p}}{i_{s}}=\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex}
  4. We have
    ip Vp = is Vs = 550 W
    Vp = 220 V
    ip = {tex}\frac{550}{220}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 A

question_html:
Draw an arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coils in a transformer with two coils on a separate limb of the core.
State the underlying principle of a transformer. Deduce the expression for the ratio of secondary voltage to the primary voltage in terms of the ratio of the number of turns of primary and secondary winding. For an ideal transformer, obtain the ratio of primary and secondary currents in terms of the ratio of the voltages in the secondary and primary voltages.
Write any two reasons for the energy losses which occur in actual transformers.
question_text: Draw an arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coils in a transformer with two coils on a separate limb of the core. State the underlying principle of a transformer. Deduce the expression for the ratio of secondary voltage to the primary voltage in terms of the ratio of the number of turns of primary and secondary winding. For an ideal transformer, obtain the ratio of primary and secondary currents in terms of the ratio of the voltages in the secondary and primary voltages. Write any two reasons for the energy losses which occur in actual transformers.
explanation: Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighbouring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input - Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Main assumptions (i) The primary resistance and current are small. (ii) The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is same / there is no leakage of flux from the core. (iii) Secondary current is small. Two reasons for energy losses Flux leakage/joule heat losses in the windings/Eddy currents/hysteresis
solution: Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighbouring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input - Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Main assumptions (i) The primary resistance and current are small. (ii) The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is same / there is no leakage of flux from the core. (iii) Secondary current is small. Two reasons for energy losses Flux leakage/joule heat losses in the windings/Eddy currents/hysteresis
solution_html:
Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighbouring coil (Principle of mutual induction)
Voltage across secondary
Vs = es = -Ns {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
Voltage across primary
Vp = ep = -Np {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, Ns > Np)
In an ideal transformer
Power Input - Power output
Ip Vp = Is Vs
{tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex}
Main assumptions
(i) The primary resistance and current are small.
(ii) The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is same / there is no leakage of flux from the core.
(iii) Secondary current is small.
Two reasons for energy losses
Flux leakage/joule heat losses in the windings/Eddy currents/hysteresis

question_html: State which of the two a capacitor or an inductor, tends to become SHORT when the frequency of the applied alternating voltage has a high value.
question_text: State which of the two a capacitor or an inductor, tends to become SHORT when the frequency of the applied alternating voltage has a high value.
explanation: Between the inductor and capacitor, the capacitor will act short at high frequencies while it will act openly at low frequencies. So at high frequencies, the capacitor has time to get a very less amount of charge while at low frequencies, there is a lot of time for the capacitor to collect a considerable amount of charge which shows the presence of large reverse voltage. Hence the sum of the voltages of capacitor and ac source will be nearly zero which shows current also to be nearly zero.
solution: Between the inductor and capacitor, the capacitor will act short at high frequencies while it will act openly at low frequencies. So at high frequencies, the capacitor has time to get a very less amount of charge while at low frequencies, there is a lot of time for the capacitor to collect a considerable amount of charge which shows the presence of large reverse voltage. Hence the sum of the voltages of capacitor and ac source will be nearly zero which shows current also to be nearly zero.
solution_html: Between the inductor and capacitor, the capacitor will act short at high frequencies while it will act openly at low frequencies. So at high frequencies, the capacitor has time to get a very less amount of charge while at low frequencies, there is a lot of time for the capacitor to collect a considerable amount of charge which shows the presence of large reverse voltage. Hence the sum of the voltages of capacitor and ac source will be nearly zero which shows current also to be nearly zero.

question_html:
A light bulb and an open coil inductor are connected to an ac source through a key as shown in the figure. The switch is closed and after some time, an iron rod is inserted into the interior of the inductor. The glow of the light bulb (a) increases, (b) decreases, (c ) is unchanged, as the iron rod is inserted.

 


Define capacitor reactance. Write its SI units.
question_text: A light bulb and an open coil inductor are connected to an ac source through a key as shown in the figure. The switch is closed and after some time, an iron rod is inserted into the interior of the inductor. The glow of the light bulb (a) increases, (b) decreases, (c ) is unchanged, as the iron rod is inserted. Define capacitor reactance. Write its SI units.
explanation: Capacitor reactance is the resistance offered by a capacitor, when it is connected to an electric circuit. It is given by {tex}{X_C} = \frac1{\omega C} = \frac 1{2\pi fC}{/tex} where {tex}\omega{/tex} is the angular frequency and C is the capacitance of capacitor. The SI unit of capacitor reactance is Ohm ( {tex}\Omega {/tex} ).
solution: Capacitor reactance is the resistance offered by a capacitor, when it is connected to an electric circuit. It is given by {tex}{X_C} = \frac1{\omega C} = \frac 1{2\pi fC}{/tex} where {tex}\omega{/tex} is the angular frequency and C is the capacitance of capacitor. The SI unit of capacitor reactance is Ohm ( {tex}\Omega {/tex} ).
solution_html:
Capacitor reactance is the resistance offered by a capacitor, when it is connected to an electric circuit. It is given by
{tex}{X_C} = \frac1{\omega C} = \frac 1{2\pi fC}{/tex}
where {tex}\omega{/tex} is the angular frequency and C is the capacitance of capacitor.
The SI unit of capacitor reactance is Ohm ({tex}\Omega {/tex}).

question_html: Calculate the value of the additional capacitor which may be joined suitably to the capacitor C that would make the power factor of the circuit unity.
question_text: Calculate the value of the additional capacitor which may be joined suitably to the capacitor C that would make the power factor of the circuit unity.
explanation: For unity power factor, X L = X c {tex}\omega L = 1 / \omega C ^ { \prime } {/tex} {tex}C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { \omega ^ { 2 } L } = \frac { 1 } { ( 1000 ) ^ { 2 } \times 100 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } = 10 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { F } = 10 \mu F{/tex} {tex}\because \quad C ^ { \prime } = C + C ^ { \prime \prime }{/tex} {tex}C ^ { \prime \prime } = C ^ { \prime } - C = 10 - 2 = 8 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} So, the required capacitance should be {tex}8 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} which will be added in parallel with the given capacitor.
solution: For unity power factor, X L = X c {tex}\omega L = 1 / \omega C ^ { \prime } {/tex} {tex}C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { \omega ^ { 2 } L } = \frac { 1 } { ( 1000 ) ^ { 2 } \times 100 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } = 10 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { F } = 10 \mu F{/tex} {tex}\because \quad C ^ { \prime } = C + C ^ { \prime \prime }{/tex} {tex}C ^ { \prime \prime } = C ^ { \prime } - C = 10 - 2 = 8 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} So, the required capacitance should be {tex}8 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} which will be added in parallel with the given capacitor.
solution_html:
For unity power factor, XL= Xc
{tex}\omega L = 1 / \omega C ^ { \prime } {/tex}
{tex}C ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { \omega ^ { 2 } L } = \frac { 1 } { ( 1000 ) ^ { 2 } \times 100 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } = 10 ^ { - 5 } \mathrm { F } = 10 \mu F{/tex}
{tex}\because \quad C ^ { \prime } = C + C ^ { \prime \prime }{/tex}
{tex}C ^ { \prime \prime } = C ^ { \prime } - C = 10 - 2 = 8 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex}
So, the required capacitance should be {tex}8 \mu \mathrm { F }{/tex} which will be added in parallel with the given capacitor.

question_html:
Derive the expression for the average power dissipated in a series LCR circuit for an ac source of a voltage, V = Vm sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}, carrying a current, i = im sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}
Hence define the term Wattless current. State under what condition it can be realized in a circuit.
question_text: Derive the expression for the average power dissipated in a series LCR circuit for an ac source of a voltage, V = V m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} , carrying a current, i = i m sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} Hence define the term Wattless current. State under what condition it can be realized in a circuit.
explanation: V = V m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} i = i m sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} Power at any instant P = Vi = V m i m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} Sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} P = {tex}\frac{V_{m} i_{m}}{2}{/tex} [cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos (2 {tex}\omega t+\phi)]{/tex} The average of the second term in the above expression is zero over a full cycle. {tex}\therefore{/tex} power dissipated in a series LCR circuit is given by = {tex}\bar{P}=\frac{V_{m} i_{m}}{2} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\bar{P}=\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{i_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\therefore \bar{P}=V_{n m s} I_{r m s} \cos \phi{/tex} Wattles's current is that which flows in the circuit but no power dissipation occurs. It is realized only when the circuit is purely inductive or capacitive, i.e., when cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\phi=\frac{\pm \pi}{2}{/tex}
solution: V = V m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} i = i m sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} Power at any instant P = Vi = V m i m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} Sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} P = {tex}\frac{V_{m} i_{m}}{2}{/tex} [cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos (2 {tex}\omega t+\phi)]{/tex} The average of the second term in the above expression is zero over a full cycle. {tex}\therefore{/tex} power dissipated in a series LCR circuit is given by = {tex}\bar{P}=\frac{V_{m} i_{m}}{2} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\bar{P}=\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{i_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\therefore \bar{P}=V_{n m s} I_{r m s} \cos \phi{/tex} Wattles's current is that which flows in the circuit but no power dissipation occurs. It is realized only when the circuit is purely inductive or capacitive, i.e., when cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\phi=\frac{\pm \pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
V = Vm sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}
i = im sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}
Power at any instant
P = Vi = Vmim sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} Sin {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}
P = {tex}\frac{V_{m} i_{m}}{2}{/tex} [cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos (2{tex}\omega t+\phi)]{/tex}
The average of the second term in the above expression is zero over a full cycle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} power dissipated in a series LCR circuit is given by = {tex}\bar{P}=\frac{V_{m} i_{m}}{2} \cos \phi{/tex}
{tex}\bar{P}=\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{i_{m}}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \bar{P}=V_{n m s} I_{r m s} \cos \phi{/tex}
Wattles's current is that which flows in the circuit but no power dissipation occurs.
It is realized only when the circuit is purely inductive or capacitive, i.e., when cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\phi=\frac{\pm \pi}{2}{/tex}

question_html: For circuits used for transporting electric power, a low power factor implies large power loss in transmission. Why?
question_text: For circuits used for transporting electric power, a low power factor implies large power loss in transmission. Why?
explanation: We know, {tex}\mathrm{P}_{a v}=\mathrm{E}_{v} \mathrm{I}_{v} \cos \phi{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}_{v}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{a v}}{\mathrm{E}_{v} \cos \phi}{/tex} To supply a given power, low power factor i.e. low value of cos {tex}\phi{/tex} means larger current will flow across the line wires. Since energy dissipation is l 2 R, large heat losses will occur in such a case.
solution: We know, {tex}\mathrm{P}_{a v}=\mathrm{E}_{v} \mathrm{I}_{v} \cos \phi{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{I}_{v}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{a v}}{\mathrm{E}_{v} \cos \phi}{/tex} To supply a given power, low power factor i.e. low value of cos {tex}\phi{/tex} means larger current will flow across the line wires. Since energy dissipation is l 2 R, large heat losses will occur in such a case.
solution_html:
We know,
{tex}\mathrm{P}_{a v}=\mathrm{E}_{v} \mathrm{I}_{v} \cos \phi{/tex}
or {tex}\mathrm{I}_{v}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{a v}}{\mathrm{E}_{v} \cos \phi}{/tex}
To supply a given power, low power factor i.e. low value of cos {tex}\phi{/tex} means larger current will flow across the line wires. Since energy dissipation is l2R, large heat losses will occur in such a case.

explanation: Power factor, {tex}\cos \phi = 0.5 = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} So, {tex}\phi{/tex} = 60 o The phase difference between voltage and current is 60° or {tex}\frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} radian.

Power factor, {tex}\cos \phi = 0.5 = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
So, {tex}\phi{/tex} = 60o
The phase difference between voltage and current is 60° or {tex}\frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} radian.
