question_html:
An a.c. voltage V = Vmsin {tex}\omega{/tex}t is applied across an inductor of inductance L Apply Kirchhoff's loop rule to obtain expressions for

  1. the current flowing in the circuit
  2. the inductive reactance L

Hence find the instantaneous power Pi supplied to the inductor. Show graphically the variation of P with {tex}\omega{/tex}t.
question_text: An a.c. voltage V = V m sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t is applied across an inductor of inductance L Apply Kirchhoff's loop rule to obtain expressions for the current flowing in the circuit the inductive reactance L Hence find the instantaneous power Pi supplied to the inductor. Show graphically the variation of P with {tex}\omega{/tex} t.
explanation: When an alternating voltage V=Vm sinwt is applied across an inductor L, an alternating current flows through it and consequently an emf is induced across its ends which is {tex}\varepsilon=\frac {-\mathrm{Ldl}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} . Thus, in equilibrium V + {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 or V - {tex}\text L \frac{\text {dl}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\text L \frac{\text {dl}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = V = V m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L} \int \sin \omega t d t=\frac{V_m}{L}\left[\frac{-\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L \omega} \cos \omega t{/tex} I m = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L \omega}=\frac{V_m}{X_L}{/tex} and X L = {tex}L_\omega{/tex} is called the inductive reactance of given inductor. Instantaneous power Pi supplied to the inductor. We can write current as \begin{aligned} i = {tex}\frac{\text V_\text m}{\omega \text L}(-\cos \omega t){/tex} or i = {tex}\frac{V_m}{\omega L} \sin (\omega t-\frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_m}{X_L} \sin (\omega t-\frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} ...(1) substituting value of I m in equation 1 i = I m sin {tex}(\omega t-\frac \pi 2){/tex}
solution: When an alternating voltage V=Vm sinwt is applied across an inductor L, an alternating current flows through it and consequently an emf is induced across its ends which is {tex}\varepsilon=\frac {-\mathrm{Ldl}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} . Thus, in equilibrium V + {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0 or V - {tex}\text L \frac{\text {dl}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\text L \frac{\text {dl}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = V = V m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L} \int \sin \omega t d t=\frac{V_m}{L}\left[\frac{-\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L \omega} \cos \omega t{/tex} I m = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L \omega}=\frac{V_m}{X_L}{/tex} and X L = {tex}L_\omega{/tex} is called the inductive reactance of given inductor. Instantaneous power Pi supplied to the inductor. We can write current as \begin{aligned} i = {tex}\frac{\text V_\text m}{\omega \text L}(-\cos \omega t){/tex} or i = {tex}\frac{V_m}{\omega L} \sin (\omega t-\frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_m}{X_L} \sin (\omega t-\frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} ...(1) substituting value of I m in equation 1 i = I m sin {tex}(\omega t-\frac \pi 2){/tex}
solution_html:
When an alternating voltage V=Vm sinwt is applied across an inductor L,

  1. an alternating current flows through it and consequently an emf is induced across its ends which is {tex}\varepsilon=\frac {-\mathrm{Ldl}}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}. Thus, in equilibrium V + {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 0
    or V - {tex}\text L \frac{\text {dl}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\text L \frac{\text {dl}}{\text {dt}}{/tex} = V = Vm sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L} \int \sin \omega t d t=\frac{V_m}{L}\left[\frac{-\cos \omega t}{\omega}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L \omega} \cos \omega t{/tex}
  2. Im = {tex}\frac{V_m}{L \omega}=\frac{V_m}{X_L}{/tex} and XL = {tex}L_\omega{/tex} is called the inductive reactance of given inductor.
    Instantaneous power Pi supplied to the inductor.
    We can write current as
    \begin{aligned}
    i = {tex}\frac{\text V_\text m}{\omega \text L}(-\cos \omega t){/tex} or
    i = {tex}\frac{V_m}{\omega L} \sin (\omega t-\frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{V_m}{X_L} \sin (\omega t-\frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} ...(1)
    substituting value of Im in equation 1
    i = Im sin {tex}(\omega t-\frac \pi 2){/tex}

question_html: Why is choke coil needed in the use of fluorescent tubes with a.c. mains? Why can we not use an ordinary resistor instead of the choke coil?
question_text: Why is choke coil needed in the use of fluorescent tubes with a.c. mains? Why can we not use an ordinary resistor instead of the choke coil?
explanation: The choke coil is used to reduce the current. As its power factor is zero, it reduces the current without wasting the power. If an ordinary resistor is used instead of the choke coil, it will waste power in the form of heat.
solution: The choke coil is used to reduce the current. As its power factor is zero, it reduces the current without wasting the power. If an ordinary resistor is used instead of the choke coil, it will waste power in the form of heat.
solution_html: The choke coil is used to reduce the current. As its power factor is zero, it reduces the current without wasting the power. If an ordinary resistor is used instead of the choke coil, it will waste power in the form of heat.

question_html: Show that the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in an AC circuit containing an ideal capacitor.
question_text: Show that the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in an AC circuit containing an ideal capacitor.
explanation: Let us consider a capacitor C connected to an AC source as shown below: Let the AC voltage applied by {tex}V = V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} ....(i) Now, {tex} V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule, we have {tex}V _ { m } \sin \omega t = \frac { q } { C } \Rightarrow q = C V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} Also, {tex}i = \frac { d q } { d t } \Rightarrow i = \frac { d } { d t } C \left( V _ { m } \sin \omega t \right){/tex} {tex}i = \omega C V _ { m } \cos \omega t{/tex} ...(ii) We know that {tex}\cos \omega t = \sin ( \omega t + \pi / 2 ){/tex} In the equation (i) {tex}V _ { m } = i _ { m } X _ { C } = i _ { m } \frac { 1 } { \omega C }{/tex} , where X C being capacitive reactance of the circuit {tex}\Rightarrow \quad i _ { m } = V _ { m } \omega C{/tex} ...(iii), where i m is the peak value of the current. Substituting the values of Eqs. (iii) in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}i = i_ { m } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ....(iv) From equations (i) and (iv) it is clear that the phase difference between voltage and current is {tex}\pi{/tex} /2. The phase diagram which shows the current lead the voltage in phase by 90 0 is given below
solution: Let us consider a capacitor C connected to an AC source as shown below: Let the AC voltage applied by {tex}V = V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} ....(i) Now, {tex} V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex} Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule, we have {tex}V _ { m } \sin \omega t = \frac { q } { C } \Rightarrow q = C V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} Also, {tex}i = \frac { d q } { d t } \Rightarrow i = \frac { d } { d t } C \left( V _ { m } \sin \omega t \right){/tex} {tex}i = \omega C V _ { m } \cos \omega t{/tex} ...(ii) We know that {tex}\cos \omega t = \sin ( \omega t + \pi / 2 ){/tex} In the equation (i) {tex}V _ { m } = i _ { m } X _ { C } = i _ { m } \frac { 1 } { \omega C }{/tex} , where X C being capacitive reactance of the circuit {tex}\Rightarrow \quad i _ { m } = V _ { m } \omega C{/tex} ...(iii), where i m is the peak value of the current. Substituting the values of Eqs. (iii) in Eq. (ii), we get {tex}i = i_ { m } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ....(iv) From equations (i) and (iv) it is clear that the phase difference between voltage and current is {tex}\pi{/tex} /2. The phase diagram which shows the current lead the voltage in phase by 90 0 is given below
solution_html:
Let us consider a capacitor C connected to an AC source as shown below:

Let the AC voltage applied by 
{tex}V = V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex}....(i)
Now, {tex} V = \frac { q } { C }{/tex}
Applying Kirchhoff's loop rule, we have
{tex}V _ { m } \sin \omega t = \frac { q } { C } \Rightarrow q = C V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex}
Also, {tex}i = \frac { d q } { d t } \Rightarrow i = \frac { d } { d t } C \left( V _ { m } \sin \omega t \right){/tex}
{tex}i = \omega C V _ { m } \cos \omega t{/tex}   ...(ii)
We know that {tex}\cos \omega t = \sin ( \omega t + \pi / 2 ){/tex}
In the equation (i)
{tex}V _ { m } = i _ { m } X _ { C } = i _ { m } \frac { 1 } { \omega C }{/tex}, where XC being capacitive reactance of the circuit
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad i _ { m } = V _ { m } \omega C{/tex}  ...(iii), where im is the peak value of the current.
Substituting the values of Eqs. (iii) in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}i = i_ { m } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}....(iv)
From equations (i) and (iv) it is clear that the phase difference between voltage and current is {tex}\pi{/tex}/2.

question_html: A capacitor is connected in series to an ammeter across a d.c. source. Why does the ammeter show a momentary deflection during the charging of the capacitor? What would be the deflection when it is fully charged?
question_text: A capacitor is connected in series to an ammeter across a d.c. source. Why does the ammeter show a momentary deflection during the charging of the capacitor? What would be the deflection when it is fully charged?
explanation: The momentary deflection is due to the transient current flowing through the circuit when the capacitor is getting charged. The deflection would be zero when the capacitor gets fully charged.
solution: The momentary deflection is due to the transient current flowing through the circuit when the capacitor is getting charged. The deflection would be zero when the capacitor gets fully charged.
solution_html:
The momentary deflection is due to the transient current flowing through the circuit when the capacitor is getting charged.
The deflection would be zero when the capacitor gets fully charged.

question_html: A series LCR circuit is connected to an a.c. source of variable frequency. Draw a suitable phasor diagram to deduce the expressions for the amplitude of the current and phase angle.
question_text: A series LCR circuit is connected to an a.c. source of variable frequency. Draw a suitable phasor diagram to deduce the expressions for the amplitude of the current and phase angle.
explanation: From the phasor diagram, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{V}}=\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{L}}+\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{R}}+\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} Magnitude of net voltage, {tex}\mathrm{V}_{m}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Rm}}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Cm}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L} m}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_{m}=I_m\sqrt{\left[\mathrm{R}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^{2}\right]}{/tex} {tex}I_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{\left[R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}\right]}}{/tex} From the figure, {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{V_{C m}-V_{L m}}{V_{R m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{I_{m}\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)}{I_{m} R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}\right){/tex}
solution: From the phasor diagram, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{V}}=\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{L}}+\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{R}}+\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} Magnitude of net voltage, {tex}\mathrm{V}_{m}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Rm}}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Cm}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L} m}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_{m}=I_m\sqrt{\left[\mathrm{R}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^{2}\right]}{/tex} {tex}I_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{\left[R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}\right]}}{/tex} From the figure, {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{V_{C m}-V_{L m}}{V_{R m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{I_{m}\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)}{I_{m} R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
From the phasor diagram,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{V}}=\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{L}}+\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{R}}+\vec{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}
Magnitude of net voltage,
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{m}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Rm}}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Cm}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L} m}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{m}=I_m\sqrt{\left[\mathrm{R}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^{2}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}I_{m}=\frac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{\left[R^{2}+\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)^{2}\right]}}{/tex}
From the figure,
{tex}\tan \phi=\frac{V_{C m}-V_{L m}}{V_{R m}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{I_{m}\left(X_{C}-X_{L}\right)}{I_{m} R}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}\right){/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
A voltage V = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied to a series LCR circuit. Derive the expression for the average power dissipated over a cycle.
Under what conditions are

  1. no power dissipated even though the current flows through the circuit,
  2. maximum power dissipated in the circuit?
question_text: A voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied to a series LCR circuit. Derive the expression for the average power dissipated over a cycle. Under what conditions are no power dissipated even though the current flows through the circuit, maximum power dissipated in the circuit?
explanation: Applied voltage = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} Current in the circuit = I 0 sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} Where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase lag of the current with respect to the voltage applied Hence instantaneous power dissipation = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} I 0 sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}=V_{0} \sin \omega t \times I_{0} \sin (\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}{/tex} [2sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} .sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} ] {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}{/tex} [cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos (2 {tex}\omega t-\phi)]{/tex} Therefore, average power for one complete cycle = average of {tex}\left[\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}\{\cos \phi-\cos (2 \omega t-\phi)\}\right]{/tex} The average of the second term over a complete cycle is zero. Hence, average power dissipated over one complete cycle = {tex}\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \cos \phi{/tex} Conditions: No power is dissipated when R = 0 (or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 90 o ) Maximum power is dissipated when X L = X C Or {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} (or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0)
solution: Applied voltage = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} Current in the circuit = I 0 sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} Where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase lag of the current with respect to the voltage applied Hence instantaneous power dissipation = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} I 0 sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}=V_{0} \sin \omega t \times I_{0} \sin (\omega t-\phi){/tex} {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}{/tex} [2sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} .sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex} ] {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}{/tex} [cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos (2 {tex}\omega t-\phi)]{/tex} Therefore, average power for one complete cycle = average of {tex}\left[\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}\{\cos \phi-\cos (2 \omega t-\phi)\}\right]{/tex} The average of the second term over a complete cycle is zero. Hence, average power dissipated over one complete cycle = {tex}\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \cos \phi{/tex} Conditions: No power is dissipated when R = 0 (or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 90 o ) Maximum power is dissipated when X L = X C Or {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} (or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0)
solution_html:
Applied voltage = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}
Current in the circuit = I0 sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex}
Where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase lag of the current with respect to the voltage applied
Hence instantaneous power dissipation
= V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} I0 sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex}
{tex}=V_{0} \sin \omega t \times I_{0} \sin (\omega t-\phi){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}{/tex} [2sin {tex}\omega t{/tex}.sin {tex}(\omega t-\phi){/tex}]
{tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}{/tex} [cos {tex}\phi{/tex} - cos (2{tex}\omega t-\phi)]{/tex}
Therefore, average power for one complete cycle = average of {tex}\left[\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2}\{\cos \phi-\cos (2 \omega t-\phi)\}\right]{/tex}
The average of the second term over a complete cycle is zero.
Hence, average power dissipated over one complete cycle = {tex}\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \cos \phi{/tex}
Conditions:

  1. No power is dissipated when R = 0 (or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 90o)
     
  2. Maximum power is dissipated when XL = XC
    Or {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} (or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0)

question_html:
1. Draw a schematic arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coil in a transformer when the two coils are wound on top of each other.
  2. State the underlying principle of a transformer and obtain the expression for the ratio of secondary to primary voltage in terms of the
    1. number of secondary and primary windings and
    2. primary and secondary currents.
  3. Write the main assumption involved in deriving the above relations.
  4. Write any two reasons due to which energy losses may occur in actual transformers.
question_text: Draw a schematic arrangement for winding of primary and secondary coil in a transformer when the two coils are wound on top of each other. State the underlying principle of a transformer and obtain the expression for the ratio of secondary to primary voltage in terms of the number of secondary and primary windings and primary and secondary currents. Write the main assumption involved in deriving the above relations. Write any two reasons due to which energy losses may occur in actual transformers.
explanation: Principle of a transformer: When an alternating current flows through the primary coil, an emf is induced in the neighbouring (secondary) coil. Or When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Let {tex}\frac{d \varphi}{d t}{/tex} be the rate of change of flux through each turn of the primary and the secondary coil {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{-N_{1} \frac{d \phi}{d t}}{-N_{2} \frac{d \phi}{d t}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}{/tex} and e 1 =v 1 and e 2 =v 2 or {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}{/tex} ...(i) But for an ideal transformer, V 1 l 1 = V 2 l 2 {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex} Main assumptions: The primary resistance and current are small. The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is the same/there is no leakage of flux from the core. Secondary current is small. Reason due to which energy losses may occur: Flux leakage/Resistance of the coils and the Eddy currents/ Hysteresis.
solution: Principle of a transformer: When an alternating current flows through the primary coil, an emf is induced in the neighbouring (secondary) coil. Or When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Let {tex}\frac{d \varphi}{d t}{/tex} be the rate of change of flux through each turn of the primary and the secondary coil {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{-N_{1} \frac{d \phi}{d t}}{-N_{2} \frac{d \phi}{d t}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}{/tex} and e 1 =v 1 and e 2 =v 2 or {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}{/tex} ...(i) But for an ideal transformer, V 1 l 1 = V 2 l 2 {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex} ...(ii) From equation (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex} Main assumptions: The primary resistance and current are small. The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is the same/there is no leakage of flux from the core. Secondary current is small. Reason due to which energy losses may occur: Flux leakage/Resistance of the coils and the Eddy currents/ Hysteresis.
solution_html:
2. Principle of a transformer: When an alternating current flows through the primary coil, an emf is induced in the neighbouring (secondary) coil. Or When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it.
    1. Let {tex}\frac{d \varphi}{d t}{/tex} be the rate of change of flux through each turn of the primary and the secondary coil
      {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{-N_{1} \frac{d \phi}{d t}}{-N_{2} \frac{d \phi}{d t}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}{/tex} and e1=v1 and e2 =v2
      or {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
    2. But for an ideal transformer,
      V1l1 = V2l2
      {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex} ...(ii)
      From equation (i) and (ii)
      {tex}\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=\frac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}{/tex}
  3. Main assumptions:
    1. The primary resistance and current are small.
    2. The flux linked with the primary and secondary coils is the same/there is no leakage of flux from the core.
    3. Secondary current is small.
  4. Reason due to which energy losses may occur: Flux leakage/Resistance of the coils and the  Eddy currents/ Hysteresis.

question_html: Why is the core of a transformer laminated?
question_text: Why is the core of a transformer laminated?
explanation: The core of the transformer is laminated for the following reasons: - to control its weight - to regulate the energy loss due to eddy currents - to increase the formation of secondary voltage
solution: The core of the transformer is laminated for the following reasons: - to control its weight - to regulate the energy loss due to eddy currents - to increase the formation of secondary voltage
solution_html:
The core of the transformer is laminated for the following reasons:
- to control its weight
- to regulate the energy loss due to eddy currents
- to increase the formation of secondary voltage

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. For a given AC, i = im sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} , show that the average power dissipated in a resistor R over a complete cycle is {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R{/tex}. 
  2. A light bulb is rated at 100 W for a 220V AC supply. Calculate the resistance of the bulb.
question_text: For a given AC, i = i m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} , show that the average power dissipated in a resistor R over a complete cycle is {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R{/tex} . A light bulb is rated at 100 W for a 220V AC supply. Calculate the resistance of the bulb.
explanation: The average power dissipated, {tex}\overline { P } = \left( i ^ { 2 } R \right) = \left( i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t \right) = i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t \right){/tex} {tex}\because \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 - \cos 2 \omega t ){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}(\because \cos 2\omega t = 0){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \overline { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R{/tex} Power of the bulb P = 100 W Voltage, V = 220 V {tex}R = \frac { V ^ { 2 } } { P } = \frac { ( 220 ) ^ { 2 } } { 100 } = 484 \Omega{/tex}
solution: The average power dissipated, {tex}\overline { P } = \left( i ^ { 2 } R \right) = \left( i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t \right) = i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t \right){/tex} {tex}\because \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 - \cos 2 \omega t ){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}(\because \cos 2\omega t = 0){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \overline { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R{/tex} Power of the bulb P = 100 W Voltage, V = 220 V {tex}R = \frac { V ^ { 2 } } { P } = \frac { ( 220 ) ^ { 2 } } { 100 } = 484 \Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The average power dissipated,
    {tex}\overline { P } = \left( i ^ { 2 } R \right) = \left( i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t \right) = i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R \left( \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t \right){/tex}
    {tex}\because \sin ^ { 2 } \omega t = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 - \cos 2 \omega t ){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}(\because \cos 2\omega t = 0){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \overline { P } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } i _ { m } ^ { 2 } R{/tex}
  2. Power of the bulb P = 100 W
    Voltage, V = 220 V
    {tex}R = \frac { V ^ { 2 } } { P } = \frac { ( 220 ) ^ { 2 } } { 100 } = 484 \Omega{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. When an AC source is connected to an ideal capacitor, then show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
  2. A lamp is connected in series with a capacitor. Predict your observations when the system is connected first across a DC and then an AC source. What happens in each case if the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced?
question_text: When an AC source is connected to an ideal capacitor, then show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero. A lamp is connected in series with a capacitor. Predict your observations when the system is connected first across a DC and then an AC source. What happens in each case if the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced?
explanation: When a source of AC is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C , the charge on it grows from zero to maximum steady value Q 0 . The energy stored in a capacitor is, {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} where, V 0 is maximum potential difference across the plates of the capacitor. The alternating voltage applied is {tex} { V } = { V } _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} and the current leads the emf by a phase angle of {tex}\pi /2{/tex} {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done over a complete cycle is, {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V Id t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \left( V_ { 0 } \sin \omega t \right) \left( I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } 2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ 1 - \frac { \cos 2 \omega t } { 2 \omega } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { T } = 0{/tex} Now as the work done is zero, hence the average power is also zero (as, time can never be zero). When a DC source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Because a condenser offers infinite resistance for a DC source and never allows a DC current to flow through it. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when capacitance of capacitor is reduced. Now when an AC source is connected with the circuit, the capacitor offers capacitive reactance {tex}X _ { C } = 1 / \omega C{/tex} (where {tex}\omega {/tex} is the angular frequency of the applied AC voltage). The current flows in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing the value of C , the capacitive reactance, X C increases. Therefore, the glowing of the bulb reduces.
solution: When a source of AC is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C , the charge on it grows from zero to maximum steady value Q 0 . The energy stored in a capacitor is, {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} where, V 0 is maximum potential difference across the plates of the capacitor. The alternating voltage applied is {tex} { V } = { V } _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} and the current leads the emf by a phase angle of {tex}\pi /2{/tex} {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done over a complete cycle is, {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V Id t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \left( V_ { 0 } \sin \omega t \right) \left( I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } 2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ 1 - \frac { \cos 2 \omega t } { 2 \omega } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { T } = 0{/tex} Now as the work done is zero, hence the average power is also zero (as, time can never be zero). When a DC source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Because a condenser offers infinite resistance for a DC source and never allows a DC current to flow through it. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when capacitance of capacitor is reduced. Now when an AC source is connected with the circuit, the capacitor offers capacitive reactance {tex}X _ { C } = 1 / \omega C{/tex} (where {tex}\omega {/tex} is the angular frequency of the applied AC voltage). The current flows in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing the value of C , the capacitive reactance, X C increases. Therefore, the glowing of the bulb reduces.
solution_html:
1. When a source of AC is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C, the charge on it grows from zero to maximum steady value Q0 . 
    The energy stored in a capacitor is, {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} where, V0 is maximum potential difference across the plates of the capacitor.
    The alternating voltage applied is
    {tex} { V } = { V } _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex}
    and the current leads the emf by a phase angle of {tex}\pi /2{/tex}
    {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done over a complete cycle is,
    {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V Id t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \left( V_ { 0 } \sin \omega t \right) \left( I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \right) d t{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } 2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t d t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex}
    {tex}W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ 1 - \frac { \cos 2 \omega t } { 2 \omega } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { T } = 0{/tex}
    Now as the work done is zero, hence the average power is also zero (as, time can never be zero).
  2. When a DC source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Because a condenser offers infinite resistance for a DC source and never allows a DC current to flow through it. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when capacitance of capacitor is reduced.
    Now when an AC source is connected with the circuit, the capacitor offers capacitive reactance {tex}X _ { C } = 1 / \omega C{/tex} (where {tex}\omega {/tex} is the angular frequency of the applied AC voltage). The current flows in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing the value of C, the capacitive reactance, XC increases. Therefore, the glowing of the bulb reduces.

question_html:

explanation: Given P = 150W, V = 220V Electric power, P = {tex}V^2 \over R{/tex} Resistance of the bulb, {tex}R = \frac { V ^ { 2 } } { P }{/tex} {tex}R = \frac { 220 \times 220 } { 150 } = 322.7 \Omega{/tex} In case of ac {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{V_{r m s}^{2}}{R}=\frac{V_{e f f}^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow R=\frac{V_{r m s}^{2}}{P_{a v}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{220 \times 220}{100}{/tex} = 484 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution: Given P = 150W, V = 220V Electric power, P = {tex}V^2 \over R{/tex} Resistance of the bulb, {tex}R = \frac { V ^ { 2 } } { P }{/tex} {tex}R = \frac { 220 \times 220 } { 150 } = 322.7 \Omega{/tex} In case of ac {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{V_{r m s}^{2}}{R}=\frac{V_{e f f}^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow R=\frac{V_{r m s}^{2}}{P_{a v}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{220 \times 220}{100}{/tex} = 484 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given P = 150W, V = 220V
    Electric power, P = {tex}V^2 \over R{/tex}
    Resistance of the bulb,
    {tex}R = \frac { V ^ { 2 } } { P }{/tex} 
    {tex}R = \frac { 220 \times 220 } { 150 } = 322.7 \Omega{/tex}
  2. In case of ac
    {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{V_{r m s}^{2}}{R}=\frac{V_{e f f}^{2}}{R}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow R=\frac{V_{r m s}^{2}}{P_{a v}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{220 \times 220}{100}{/tex}
    = 484 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

question_html:
An alternating voltage given by V = 140 sin 314t is connected across a pure resistor of 50{tex}\Omega {/tex}. Find

  1. the frequency of the source.
  2. the rms current through the resistor.
question_text: An alternating voltage given by V = 140 sin 314t is connected across a pure resistor of 50 {tex}\Omega {/tex} . Find the frequency of the source. the rms current through the resistor.
explanation: V = 140 sin 314 t Comparing it with V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} , Here, {tex}\omega = 314 \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} i.e. {tex}2 \pi f = 314{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f = 314 / 2 \pi{/tex} {tex}f = \frac { 314 } { 2 \times 3.14 }{/tex} = 50 Hz We know, {tex}I _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { V _ { \mathrm { rms } } } { R } \text { and } V _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} Here, {tex}V _ { 0 } = 140 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}V _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { 140 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = 70 \sqrt { 2 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { \operatorname { rms } } = \frac { 70 \sqrt { 2 } } { R } = \frac { 70 \sqrt { 2 } } { 50 } = 1.9 \mathrm { A } \text { } \mathrm { }{/tex}
solution: V = 140 sin 314 t Comparing it with V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} , Here, {tex}\omega = 314 \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex} i.e. {tex}2 \pi f = 314{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f = 314 / 2 \pi{/tex} {tex}f = \frac { 314 } { 2 \times 3.14 }{/tex} = 50 Hz We know, {tex}I _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { V _ { \mathrm { rms } } } { R } \text { and } V _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} Here, {tex}V _ { 0 } = 140 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}V _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { 140 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = 70 \sqrt { 2 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { \operatorname { rms } } = \frac { 70 \sqrt { 2 } } { R } = \frac { 70 \sqrt { 2 } } { 50 } = 1.9 \mathrm { A } \text { } \mathrm { }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. V = 140 sin 314 t
    Comparing it with V = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex},
    Here, {tex}\omega = 314 \mathrm { rad } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}
    i.e. {tex}2 \pi f = 314{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad f = 314 / 2 \pi{/tex}
    {tex}f = \frac { 314 } { 2 \times 3.14 }{/tex} = 50 Hz
  2. We know, {tex}I _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { V _ { \mathrm { rms } } } { R } \text { and } V _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
    Here, {tex}V _ { 0 } = 140 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
    {tex}V _ { \mathrm { rms } } = \frac { 140 } { \sqrt { 2 } } = 70 \sqrt { 2 } \mathrm { V }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad I _ { \operatorname { rms } } = \frac { 70 \sqrt { 2 } } { R } = \frac { 70 \sqrt { 2 } } { 50 } = 1.9 \mathrm { A } \text { } \mathrm { }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: State the underlying principle of a transformer. How is the large scale transmission of electric energy over long distances done with the use of transformers?
question_text: State the underlying principle of a transformer. How is the large scale transmission of electric energy over long distances done with the use of transformers?
explanation: A transformer is based on the principle of mutual induction, i.e. whenever the amount of magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the neighbouring coil. This changing flux sets up an induced emf in the secondary coil, also self-induced emf in primary coil. Electric power is transmitted over long distances at high voltage. So, step-up transformers are used at power stations to increase the voltage whereas a series of step-down transformers at customer end are used to decrease the voltage upto 220 V.
solution: A transformer is based on the principle of mutual induction, i.e. whenever the amount of magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the neighbouring coil. This changing flux sets up an induced emf in the secondary coil, also self-induced emf in primary coil. Electric power is transmitted over long distances at high voltage. So, step-up transformers are used at power stations to increase the voltage whereas a series of step-down transformers at customer end are used to decrease the voltage upto 220 V.
solution_html:
A transformer is based on the principle of mutual induction, i.e. whenever the amount of magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the neighbouring coil. This changing flux sets up an induced emf in the secondary coil, also self-induced emf in primary coil.
Electric power is transmitted over long distances at high voltage. So, step-up transformers are used at power stations to increase the voltage whereas a series of step-down transformers at customer end are used to decrease the voltage upto 220 V.

question_html: What is the function of a step-up transformer?
question_text: What is the function of a step-up transformer?
explanation: A transformer that increases voltage from primary to secondary having more secondary winding turns than primary winding turns is called a step-up transformer.
solution: A transformer that increases voltage from primary to secondary having more secondary winding turns than primary winding turns is called a step-up transformer.
solution_html: A transformer that increases voltage from primary to secondary having more secondary winding turns than primary winding turns is called a step-up transformer.

question_html: A power transmission line feeds power at 2200 V with a current of 5 A to step down transformer with its primary winding having 4000 turns. Calculate the number of turns and the current in the secondary in order to get output power at 220 V.
question_text: A power transmission line feeds power at 2200 V with a current of 5 A to step down transformer with its primary winding having 4000 turns. Calculate the number of turns and the current in the secondary in order to get output power at 220 V.
explanation: V p = 2200V, I p = 5A, N p = 4000 V s = 220 V, N s = ? l s = ? We know that, {tex}\frac { V _ { s } } { V _ { p } } = \frac { I _ { p } } { I _ { s } } =\frac {N_s}{N_p}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac { 220 } { 2200 } = \frac { 5 } { I _ { s } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 10 } = \frac { 5 } { I _ { s } } \Rightarrow I _ { s } = 50 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { I _ { p } } { I _ { s } } = \frac { N _ { s } } { N _ { p } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 5 } { 50 } = \frac { N _ { s } } { 4000 } \Rightarrow N _ { s } = 400{/tex} turns
solution: V p = 2200V, I p = 5A, N p = 4000 V s = 220 V, N s = ? l s = ? We know that, {tex}\frac { V _ { s } } { V _ { p } } = \frac { I _ { p } } { I _ { s } } =\frac {N_s}{N_p}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac { 220 } { 2200 } = \frac { 5 } { I _ { s } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 10 } = \frac { 5 } { I _ { s } } \Rightarrow I _ { s } = 50 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac { I _ { p } } { I _ { s } } = \frac { N _ { s } } { N _ { p } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 5 } { 50 } = \frac { N _ { s } } { 4000 } \Rightarrow N _ { s } = 400{/tex} turns
solution_html:
Vp = 2200V, Ip = 5A, Np = 4000
Vs = 220 V, Ns = ? ls = ?
We know that,
{tex}\frac { V _ { s } } { V _ { p } } = \frac { I _ { p } } { I _ { s } } =\frac {N_s}{N_p}{/tex}
So,
{tex}\frac { 220 } { 2200 } = \frac { 5 } { I _ { s } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 10 } = \frac { 5 } { I _ { s } } \Rightarrow I _ { s } = 50 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
Also,
{tex}\frac { I _ { p } } { I _ { s } } = \frac { N _ { s } } { N _ { p } }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 5 } { 50 } = \frac { N _ { s } } { 4000 } \Rightarrow N _ { s } = 400{/tex} turns

subject: Physics
question_html: Mention various energy losses in a transformer.
question_text: Mention various energy losses in a transformer.
explanation: The various losses in a transformer are: (i) Flux Leakage: There is always some flux leakage; that is, not all of the flux due to primary passes through the secondary due to poor design of the core or the air gaps in the core. It can be reduced by winding the primary and secondary coils one over the other. (ii) Resistance of the windings: The wire used for the winding has some resistance and so, energy is lost due to heat produced in the wire (I 2 R). In high current, low voltage windings, these are minimised by using thick wire. (iii) Eddy currents : The alternating magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the iron core and causes heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core. (iv) Hysteresis: The magnetisation of the core is repeatedly reversed by the alternating magnetic field. The resulting expenditure of energy in the core appears as heat and is kept to a minimum by using a magnetic material which has a low hysteresis loss.
solution: The various losses in a transformer are: (i) Flux Leakage: There is always some flux leakage; that is, not all of the flux due to primary passes through the secondary due to poor design of the core or the air gaps in the core. It can be reduced by winding the primary and secondary coils one over the other. (ii) Resistance of the windings: The wire used for the winding has some resistance and so, energy is lost due to heat produced in the wire (I 2 R). In high current, low voltage windings, these are minimised by using thick wire. (iii) Eddy currents : The alternating magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the iron core and causes heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core. (iv) Hysteresis: The magnetisation of the core is repeatedly reversed by the alternating magnetic field. The resulting expenditure of energy in the core appears as heat and is kept to a minimum by using a magnetic material which has a low hysteresis loss.
solution_html:
The various losses in a transformer are:
(i) Flux Leakage: There is always some flux leakage; that is, not all of the flux due to primary passes through the secondary due to poor design of the core or the air gaps in the core. It can be reduced by winding the primary and secondary coils one over the other.
(ii) Resistance of the windings: The wire used for the winding has some resistance and so, energy is lost due to heat produced in the wire (I2R). In high current, low voltage windings, these are minimised by using thick wire.
(iii) Eddy currents: The alternating magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the iron core and causes heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core.
(iv) Hysteresis: The magnetisation of the core is repeatedly reversed by the alternating magnetic field. The resulting expenditure of energy in the core appears as heat and is kept to a minimum by using a magnetic material which has a low hysteresis loss.

question_html:
The current flowing through a pure inductance 2mH is, I = 15 cos(300t) A. What is the

  1. rms and 
  2. average value of current for a complete cycle?
question_text: The current flowing through a pure inductance 2mH is, I = 15 cos(300t) A. What is the rms and average value of current for a complete cycle?
explanation: Given, current flowing through the inductor, I = 15 cos(300t) Comparing with {tex}I = {I_0}\cos \,\omega t{/tex} Here, peak value of current, I 0 = 15A For complete cycle, rms value of current, {tex}I _ { \operatorname { rms } } = \frac { I _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 15 } { \sqrt { 2 } } A{/tex} For complete cycle, average value of current is zero. Because we know that the integration value of {tex}\text {cos} ~\omega t~dt{/tex} for limits 0 to T(over complete cycle) is always zero. i.e. {tex}{I_{av}}=0{/tex}
solution: Given, current flowing through the inductor, I = 15 cos(300t) Comparing with {tex}I = {I_0}\cos \,\omega t{/tex} Here, peak value of current, I 0 = 15A For complete cycle, rms value of current, {tex}I _ { \operatorname { rms } } = \frac { I _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 15 } { \sqrt { 2 } } A{/tex} For complete cycle, average value of current is zero. Because we know that the integration value of {tex}\text {cos} ~\omega t~dt{/tex} for limits 0 to T(over complete cycle) is always zero. i.e. {tex}{I_{av}}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, current flowing through the inductor, I = 15 cos(300t)
Comparing with {tex}I = {I_0}\cos \,\omega t{/tex}
Here, peak value of current, I0 = 15A

  1. For complete cycle, rms value of current,
    {tex}I _ { \operatorname { rms } } = \frac { I _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } } = \frac { 15 } { \sqrt { 2 } } A{/tex}
  2. For complete cycle, average value of current is zero. Because we know that the integration value of {tex}\text {cos} ~\omega t~dt{/tex} for limits 0 to T(over complete cycle) is always zero.
    i.e. {tex}{I_{av}}=0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Prove that an ideal capacitor in an AC circuit does not dissipate power.
question_text: Prove that an ideal capacitor in an AC circuit does not dissipate power.
explanation: Since, average power consumption in an AC circuit is given by {tex}{P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \phi {/tex} But in pure capacitive circuit, the ac currect leads the ac voltage by a phase difference given by, {tex}\phi = \pi / 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore {P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P _ { av } = 0 \quad \left( \because \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 0 \right){/tex} Thus, no power is consumed in a pure capacitive AC circuit. That's why the ac current flowing through a pure capacitive circuit is known as 'wattless current'.
solution: Since, average power consumption in an AC circuit is given by {tex}{P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \phi {/tex} But in pure capacitive circuit, the ac currect leads the ac voltage by a phase difference given by, {tex}\phi = \pi / 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore {P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P _ { av } = 0 \quad \left( \because \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 0 \right){/tex} Thus, no power is consumed in a pure capacitive AC circuit. That's why the ac current flowing through a pure capacitive circuit is known as 'wattless current'.
solution_html:
Since, average power consumption in an AC circuit is given by
{tex}{P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \phi {/tex}
But in pure capacitive circuit, the ac currect leads the ac voltage by a phase difference given by,
{tex}\phi = \pi / 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {P_{av}} = {V_{rms}} \times {I_{rms}} \times \cos \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad P _ { av } = 0 \quad \left( \because \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } = 0 \right){/tex}
Thus, no power is consumed in a pure capacitive AC circuit. That's why the ac current flowing through a pure capacitive circuit is known as 'wattless current'.

question_html: A step-up transformer converts a low input voltage into a high output voltage. Does it violate law of conservation of energy? Explain.
question_text: A step-up transformer converts a low input voltage into a high output voltage. Does it violate law of conservation of energy? Explain.
explanation: No, it does not violate law of conservation of energy. In an ideal transformer, the output power is always equal to the input power i.e. E s I s = E p l p Therefore, in a step-up transformer, if we gain in voltage, we lose in the current.
solution: No, it does not violate law of conservation of energy. In an ideal transformer, the output power is always equal to the input power i.e. E s I s = E p l p Therefore, in a step-up transformer, if we gain in voltage, we lose in the current.
solution_html:
No, it does not violate law of conservation of energy. In an ideal transformer, the output power is always equal to the input power i.e.
Es Is = Ep lp 
Therefore, in a step-up transformer, if we gain in voltage, we lose in the current.

question_html: Why can't the transformer be used to step up d.c. voltage?
question_text: Why can't the transformer be used to step up d.c. voltage?
explanation: The d.c. supply does not produce a changing magnetic flux in the primary and hence no emf is set up in the secondary of the transformer.
solution: The d.c. supply does not produce a changing magnetic flux in the primary and hence no emf is set up in the secondary of the transformer.
solution_html: The d.c. supply does not produce a changing magnetic flux in the primary and hence no emf is set up in the secondary of the transformer.

subject: Physics
question_html: Prove that in a series LCR circuit, the power dissipated depends not only on the voltage and current but also on the cosine of the phase angle {tex}\phi{/tex} between these two.
question_text: Prove that in a series LCR circuit, the power dissipated depends not only on the voltage and current but also on the cosine of the phase angle {tex}\phi{/tex} between these two.
explanation: The emf of an LCR circuit is {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} . The impedance of a series LCR circuit is given as, Z = {tex}\sqrt{\left[R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right]}{/tex} The power factor in a series LCR circuit is given as, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} The power dissipated in the circuit is given as, P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mathrm{R}}{\left[\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\omega \mathrm{L}-\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}\right)^2\right]} {/tex} Thus, the power dissipated in the series LCR circuit is {tex}\frac{\varepsilon^2 R}{\left[R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right]}{/tex}
solution: The emf of an LCR circuit is {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} . The impedance of a series LCR circuit is given as, Z = {tex}\sqrt{\left[R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right]}{/tex} The power factor in a series LCR circuit is given as, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} The power dissipated in the circuit is given as, P = V rms I rms cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mathrm{R}}{\left[\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\omega \mathrm{L}-\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}\right)^2\right]} {/tex} Thus, the power dissipated in the series LCR circuit is {tex}\frac{\varepsilon^2 R}{\left[R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right]}{/tex}
solution_html:
The emf of an LCR circuit is {tex}\varepsilon{/tex}.
The impedance of a series LCR circuit is given as,
Z = {tex}\sqrt{\left[R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right]}{/tex}
The power factor in a series LCR circuit is given as,
cos{tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex}
The power dissipated in the circuit is given as,
P = Vrms Irms cos {tex}\phi{/tex}
= {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\varepsilon}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}}{|\mathrm{Z}|}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\varepsilon^2 \mathrm{R}}{\left[\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\omega \mathrm{L}-\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}\right)^2\right]} {/tex}
Thus, the power dissipated in the series LCR
circuit is {tex}\frac{\varepsilon^2 R}{\left[R^2+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2\right]}{/tex}

question_html:
A reactive element in an AC circuit causes the current flowing

  1. to lead in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} w.r.t. the applied voltage.
  2. to lag in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} w.r.t. the applied voltage.
    Identify the element in each case.
question_text: A reactive element in an AC circuit causes the current flowing to lead in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} w.r.t. the applied voltage. to lag in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} w.r.t. the applied voltage. Identify the element in each case.
explanation: In case when a pure capacitive circuit is connected with an AC source, the current leads in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} with respect to the applied voltage. So, the element here in the question is a capacitor. In case when a pure inductive circuit is connected with an AC source, the current lags in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} with respect to the applied voltage. So, the element here in the question is an inductor.
solution: In case when a pure capacitive circuit is connected with an AC source, the current leads in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} with respect to the applied voltage. So, the element here in the question is a capacitor. In case when a pure inductive circuit is connected with an AC source, the current lags in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} with respect to the applied voltage. So, the element here in the question is an inductor.
solution_html:
1. In case when a pure capacitive circuit is connected with an AC source, the current leads in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} with respect to the applied voltage. So, the element here in the question is a capacitor.
  2. In case when a pure inductive circuit is connected with an AC source, the current lags in phase by {tex}\pi /2{/tex} with respect to the applied voltage. So, the element here in the question is an inductor.

subject: Physics
question_html:
An AC voltage V = V0 sin {tex}\omega {/tex}t is applied across a pure inductor L. Obtain an expression for the current I in the circuit and hence obtain the

  1. inductive reactance of the circuit and
  2. the phase of the current flowing with respect to the applied voltage.
question_text: An AC voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega {/tex} t is applied across a pure inductor L. Obtain an expression for the current I in the circuit and hence obtain the inductive reactance of the circuit and the phase of the current flowing with respect to the applied voltage.
explanation: Let an alternating voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} V 0 being peak value of the AC voltage is applied across pure inductor of inductance L. The magnitude of induced emf is given by {tex}e = L d I / d t{/tex} where L and dI/dt are the inductance of the inductor and time rate of change of the current respectively. For the circuit, Magnitude of induced emf = Applied voltage i.e. {tex}L \frac { d I } { d t } = V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t \quad \text { or } \quad d I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \sin \omega t d t{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \int \sin \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \left( \frac { - \cos \omega t } { \omega } \right){/tex} or {tex}I = - \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \omega L } \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \omega L } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \omega t \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { L } } \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = I_o \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} where, I 0 = peak value of current of AC Considering equation, {tex}I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { L } } \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}X _ { L } = \omega L{/tex} = inductive reactance of the given circuit. ​ Now, {tex}V = V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I = I_o \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Thus, AC current lags behind the voltage by phase {tex}\pi /2{/tex} .
solution: Let an alternating voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} V 0 being peak value of the AC voltage is applied across pure inductor of inductance L. The magnitude of induced emf is given by {tex}e = L d I / d t{/tex} where L and dI/dt are the inductance of the inductor and time rate of change of the current respectively. For the circuit, Magnitude of induced emf = Applied voltage i.e. {tex}L \frac { d I } { d t } = V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t \quad \text { or } \quad d I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \sin \omega t d t{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \int \sin \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \left( \frac { - \cos \omega t } { \omega } \right){/tex} or {tex}I = - \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \omega L } \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \omega L } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \omega t \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { L } } \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = I_o \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} where, I 0 = peak value of current of AC Considering equation, {tex}I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { L } } \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}X _ { L } = \omega L{/tex} = inductive reactance of the given circuit. ​ Now, {tex}V = V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I = I_o \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} Thus, AC current lags behind the voltage by phase {tex}\pi /2{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let an alternating voltage, V = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} 
V0 being peak value of the AC voltage is applied across pure inductor of inductance L. The magnitude of induced emf is given by
{tex}e = L d I / d t{/tex} 
where L and dI/dt are the inductance of the inductor and time rate of change of the current respectively.
For the  circuit,
Magnitude of induced emf = Applied voltage
i.e. {tex}L \frac { d I } { d t } = V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t \quad \text { or } \quad d I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \sin \omega t d t{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \int \sin \omega t d t{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } } { L } \left( \frac { - \cos \omega t } { \omega } \right){/tex}
or {tex}I = - \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \omega L } \cos \omega t{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { V _ { 0 } } { \omega L } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \omega t \right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { L } } \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = I_o \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
where, I0 = peak value of current of AC

  1. Considering equation, {tex}I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { L } } \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
    {tex}X _ { L } = \omega L{/tex} = inductive reactance of the given circuit.​
  2. Now, {tex}V = V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I = I_o \sin \left( \omega t - \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
    Thus, AC current lags behind the voltage by phase {tex}\pi /2{/tex}.
