question_html:
An AC voltage, V = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied across a pure capacitor, C. Obtain an expression for the current I in the circuit and hence obtain the

  1. capacitive reactance of the circuit and
  2. the phase of the current flowing with respect to the applied voltage.
question_text: An AC voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} is applied across a pure capacitor, C. Obtain an expression for the current I in the circuit and hence obtain the capacitive reactance of the circuit and the phase of the current flowing with respect to the applied voltage.
explanation: Let an alternating voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} ....(a) is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C. At any instant, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to this applied ac voltage. V = Potential difference across the capacitor {tex}= \frac { q } { C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q = C V \text { or } q = C V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} Again we know that, the current I = {tex}\frac { d q } { d t } = \omega C V _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \text { or } I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } \cos \omega t{/tex} , {tex}\frac {1}{\omega C} {/tex} is the capacitive reactance offered by the capacitor. or {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ....(b) where, {tex}I _ { 0 } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { C } }{/tex} Thus, Capacitive reactance, {tex}X _ { c } = \frac { 1 } { \omega C }{/tex} From the equations (a) and (b), current leads the voltage by phase {tex}\pi / 2{/tex} .
solution: Let an alternating voltage, V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} ....(a) is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C. At any instant, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to this applied ac voltage. V = Potential difference across the capacitor {tex}= \frac { q } { C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q = C V \text { or } q = C V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} Again we know that, the current I = {tex}\frac { d q } { d t } = \omega C V _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \text { or } I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } \cos \omega t{/tex} , {tex}\frac {1}{\omega C} {/tex} is the capacitive reactance offered by the capacitor. or {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ....(b) where, {tex}I _ { 0 } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { C } }{/tex} Thus, Capacitive reactance, {tex}X _ { c } = \frac { 1 } { \omega C }{/tex} From the equations (a) and (b), current leads the voltage by phase {tex}\pi / 2{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Let an alternating voltage, V = V0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} ....(a)
    is applied across a capacitor of capacitance C. At any instant, the potential difference across the capacitor is equal to this applied ac voltage.

    V = Potential difference across the capacitor {tex}= \frac { q } { C }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow q = C V \text { or } q = C V _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex}  
    Again we know that, the current I = {tex}\frac { d q } { d t } = \omega C V _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \text { or } I = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } \cos \omega t{/tex}, {tex}\frac {1}{\omega C} {/tex}is the capacitive reactance offered by the capacitor.
    or {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}....(b)
    where, {tex}I _ { 0 } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { ( 1 / \omega C ) } = \frac { V _ { 0 } } { X _ { C } }{/tex}
    Thus, Capacitive reactance, {tex}X _ { c } = \frac { 1 } { \omega C }{/tex}
  2. From the equations (a) and (b), current leads the voltage by phase {tex}\pi / 2{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A step-up down transformer operated on a 2.5 kV line. It supplies a load with 20 A. The ratio of the primary winding to the secondary is 10 : 1. If the transformer is 90% efficient, calculate

  1. the power output
  2. the voltage and
  3. the current in the secondary coil.
question_text: A step-up down transformer operated on a 2.5 kV line. It supplies a load with 20 A. The ratio of the primary winding to the secondary is 10 : 1. If the transformer is 90% efficient, calculate the power output the voltage and the current in the secondary coil.
explanation: Given: {tex}V _ { P } = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} , Input current, {tex}I _ { P } = 20 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{N _ { p }} {N _ { s}}= \frac{10 }{1}{/tex} Output Power = {tex}\frac{{90}}{{100}} \times \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times (20{\text{)}} = 4.5 \times {10^4}{\text{W}}{/tex} {tex}\frac {V_s}{V_p} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \Rightarrow {V_s} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \times {V_p}{/tex} Voltage, {tex}V _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } = 250 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}V _ { \mathrm { s } } I _ { \mathrm { s } } = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } \mathrm { W }{/tex} Current, {tex}I _ { S } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { V _ { S } } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { 250 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { S } = 180 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}V _ { P } = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} , Input current, {tex}I _ { P } = 20 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{N _ { p }} {N _ { s}}= \frac{10 }{1}{/tex} Output Power = {tex}\frac{{90}}{{100}} \times \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times (20{\text{)}} = 4.5 \times {10^4}{\text{W}}{/tex} {tex}\frac {V_s}{V_p} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \Rightarrow {V_s} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \times {V_p}{/tex} Voltage, {tex}V _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } = 250 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}V _ { \mathrm { s } } I _ { \mathrm { s } } = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } \mathrm { W }{/tex} Current, {tex}I _ { S } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { V _ { S } } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { 250 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { S } = 180 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given: {tex}V _ { P } = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex}, Input current, {tex}I _ { P } = 20 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
    Also, {tex}\frac{N _ { p }} {N _ { s}}= \frac{10 }{1}{/tex}
    Output Power = {tex}\frac{{90}}{{100}} \times \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times (20{\text{)}} = 4.5 \times {10^4}{\text{W}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac {V_s}{V_p} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \Rightarrow {V_s} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \times {V_p}{/tex}
    Voltage, {tex}V _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } = 250 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
  3. {tex}V _ { \mathrm { s } } I _ { \mathrm { s } } = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } \mathrm { W }{/tex}
    Current, {tex}I _ { S } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { V _ { S } } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { 250 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { S } = 180 \mathrm { A }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Write any two factors responsible for energy losses in actual transformers.
question_text: Write any two factors responsible for energy losses in actual transformers.
explanation: Two factors for energy losses are The resistance of winding causes loss of energy. The eddy current causes heat loss.
solution: Two factors for energy losses are The resistance of winding causes loss of energy. The eddy current causes heat loss.
solution_html:
Two factors for energy losses are

  1. The resistance of winding causes loss of energy.
  2. The eddy current causes heat loss.

subject: Physics
question_html: How much average power over a complete cycle, does an AC source supply to a capacitor?
question_text: How much average power over a complete cycle, does an AC source supply to a capacitor?
explanation: Average power, {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { rm s } \cos \phi{/tex} But for pure capacitive circuit, {tex}\phi = 90 ^ { \circ } \text { or } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \mathrm { rad }{/tex} {tex}{P_{{\text{av}}}} = {V_{{\text{rms}}}} {I_{{\text{rms}}}} \cos {90^0} = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad P _ { \mathrm { av } } = 0{/tex}
solution: Average power, {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { rm s } \cos \phi{/tex} But for pure capacitive circuit, {tex}\phi = 90 ^ { \circ } \text { or } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \mathrm { rad }{/tex} {tex}{P_{{\text{av}}}} = {V_{{\text{rms}}}} {I_{{\text{rms}}}} \cos {90^0} = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad P _ { \mathrm { av } } = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Average power, {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { rm s } \cos \phi{/tex} 
But for pure capacitive circuit, {tex}\phi = 90 ^ { \circ } \text { or } \frac { \pi } { 2 } \mathrm { rad }{/tex}
{tex}{P_{{\text{av}}}} = {V_{{\text{rms}}}} {I_{{\text{rms}}}} \cos {90^0} = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad P _ { \mathrm { av } } = 0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An AC current, I = I0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} produces certain heat H in a resistor R over a time {tex}T\, = 2\pi /\omega {/tex}. Write the value of the DC current that would produce the same heat in the same resistor in the same time.
question_text: An AC current, I = I 0 sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} produces certain heat H in a resistor R over a time {tex}T\, = 2\pi /\omega {/tex} . Write the value of the DC current that would produce the same heat in the same resistor in the same time.
explanation: Heat produced by DC is H = I 2 RT ....(i) Heat produced by AC is {tex}H = I _ { V } ^ { 2 } R T \quad \text { or } \quad H = \left( \frac { I _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } R T{/tex} ....(ii) where I V = I 0 / {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = rms value of the AC current From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, {tex}I ^ { 2 } R T = \frac { I _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } R T } { 2 } \text { or } I = I _ { 0 } / \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} where I stands for DC and I 0 is the peak value of AC current.
solution: Heat produced by DC is H = I 2 RT ....(i) Heat produced by AC is {tex}H = I _ { V } ^ { 2 } R T \quad \text { or } \quad H = \left( \frac { I _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } R T{/tex} ....(ii) where I V = I 0 / {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = rms value of the AC current From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, {tex}I ^ { 2 } R T = \frac { I _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } R T } { 2 } \text { or } I = I _ { 0 } / \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} where I stands for DC and I 0 is the peak value of AC current.
solution_html:
Heat produced by DC is H = I2RT ....(i)
Heat produced by AC is 
{tex}H = I _ { V } ^ { 2 } R T \quad \text { or } \quad H = \left( \frac { I _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } R T{/tex} ....(ii)
where IV = I0/{tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = rms value of the AC current
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get,
{tex}I ^ { 2 } R T = \frac { I _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } R T } { 2 } \text { or } I = I _ { 0 } / \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
where I stands for DC and I0 is the peak value of AC current.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. An alternating voltage V = Vm  sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} applied to a series  L-C-R  circuit derives a current given by
    I = Im sin{tex}(\omega t + \phi ).{/tex} Deduce an expression for the average power dissipated over a cycle.
  2. For circuit used for transporting electric power, a low power factor implies large power loss in transmission. Explain.
question_text: An alternating voltage V = V m sin {tex}\omega t{/tex} applied to a series L-C-R circuit derives a current given by I = I m sin {tex}(\omega t + \phi ).{/tex} Deduce an expression for the average power dissipated over a cycle. For circuit used for transporting electric power, a low power factor implies large power loss in transmission. Explain.
explanation: Let at any instant, the current and voltage in an L-C-R series AC circuit is given by {tex}V = V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I = I _ { m } \sin ( \omega t + \phi ){/tex} where V m and I m are the peak values of the ac voltage and ac current respectively. The instantaneous power is given by {tex}P = V I = I _ { m } \sin ( \omega t + \phi ) V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ 2 \sin \omega t \sin ( \omega t + \phi ) ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore P = V I = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ \cos \phi - \cos ( 2 \omega t + \phi ) ] \quad \ldots ( i ){/tex} {tex}[ \because 2 \sin A \sin B = \cos ( A - B ) - \cos ( A + B ) ]{/tex} Work done for a very small time interval dt is given by {tex}d W = P d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d W = VI d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total work done over a complete cycle i.e. from 0 to T is given by, {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t{/tex} But {tex}P _ { a v } = \frac { W } { T } = \frac { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t } { T }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P _ { a v } = \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ \cos \phi - \cos ( 2 \omega t + \phi) ] d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{V_m}{I_m}}}{{2T}}\left[ {\int_0^T {\cos } \phi dt - \int_0^T {\cos } (2\omega t + \phi )dt} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 T } [ \cos \phi (t ) ] _ { 0 } ^ { T } - 0\;( B y \text { trigonometry) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 T } \cos \phi \times T = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } \cos \phi{/tex} {tex} = \frac { V _ { m } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \times \frac { I _ { m } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P _ { a v } = V _ { r ms} I _ { r m s } \cos \phi{/tex} This is the required expression. Power factor, cos {tex}\phi = \frac { R } { Z }{/tex} where, R = resistance and Z = impedance of the circuit. Low power factor (cos {tex}\phi {/tex} ) implies lower ohmic resistance which implies larger power loss in power system (transmission line), because in power system power, P {tex}\propto\frac {1}{R}{/tex} .
solution: Let at any instant, the current and voltage in an L-C-R series AC circuit is given by {tex}V = V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I = I _ { m } \sin ( \omega t + \phi ){/tex} where V m and I m are the peak values of the ac voltage and ac current respectively. The instantaneous power is given by {tex}P = V I = I _ { m } \sin ( \omega t + \phi ) V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ 2 \sin \omega t \sin ( \omega t + \phi ) ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore P = V I = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ \cos \phi - \cos ( 2 \omega t + \phi ) ] \quad \ldots ( i ){/tex} {tex}[ \because 2 \sin A \sin B = \cos ( A - B ) - \cos ( A + B ) ]{/tex} Work done for a very small time interval dt is given by {tex}d W = P d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d W = VI d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total work done over a complete cycle i.e. from 0 to T is given by, {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t{/tex} But {tex}P _ { a v } = \frac { W } { T } = \frac { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t } { T }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P _ { a v } = \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ \cos \phi - \cos ( 2 \omega t + \phi) ] d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{V_m}{I_m}}}{{2T}}\left[ {\int_0^T {\cos } \phi dt - \int_0^T {\cos } (2\omega t + \phi )dt} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 T } [ \cos \phi (t ) ] _ { 0 } ^ { T } - 0\;( B y \text { trigonometry) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 T } \cos \phi \times T = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } \cos \phi{/tex} {tex} = \frac { V _ { m } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \times \frac { I _ { m } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow P _ { a v } = V _ { r ms} I _ { r m s } \cos \phi{/tex} This is the required expression. Power factor, cos {tex}\phi = \frac { R } { Z }{/tex} where, R = resistance and Z = impedance of the circuit. Low power factor (cos {tex}\phi {/tex} ) implies lower ohmic resistance which implies larger power loss in power system (transmission line), because in power system power, P {tex}\propto\frac {1}{R}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Let at any instant, the current and voltage in an L-C-R series AC circuit is given by
    {tex}V = V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex} and
    {tex}I = I _ { m } \sin ( \omega t + \phi ){/tex}
    where Vm and Im are the peak values of the ac voltage and ac current respectively.
    The instantaneous power is given by 
    {tex}P = V I = I _ { m } \sin ( \omega t + \phi ) V _ { m } \sin \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow P = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ 2 \sin \omega t \sin ( \omega t + \phi ) ]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore P = V I = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ \cos \phi - \cos ( 2 \omega t + \phi ) ] \quad \ldots ( i ){/tex}
    {tex}[ \because 2 \sin A \sin B = \cos ( A - B ) - \cos ( A + B ) ]{/tex}
    Work done for a very small time interval dt is given by
    {tex}d W = P d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow d W = VI d t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Total work done over a complete cycle i.e. from 0 to T is given by, 
    {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t{/tex}
    But {tex}P _ { a v } = \frac { W } { T } = \frac { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t } { T }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad P _ { a v } = \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V I d t{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } [ \cos \phi - \cos ( 2 \omega t + \phi) ] d t{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac{{{V_m}{I_m}}}{{2T}}\left[ {\int_0^T {\cos } \phi dt - \int_0^T {\cos } (2\omega t + \phi )dt} \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 T } [ \cos \phi (t ) ] _ { 0 } ^ { T } - 0\;( B y \text { trigonometry) }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 T } \cos \phi \times T = \frac { V _ { m } I _ { m } } { 2 } \cos \phi{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac { V _ { m } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \times \frac { I _ { m } } { \sqrt { 2 } } \cos \phi{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow P _ { a v } = V _ { r ms} I _ { r m s } \cos \phi{/tex}
    This is the required expression.

  2. Power factor, cos{tex}\phi = \frac { R } { Z }{/tex}
    where, R = resistance and Z = impedance of the circuit.
    Low power factor (cos {tex}\phi {/tex}) implies lower ohmic resistance which implies larger power loss in power system (transmission line), because in power system power, P {tex}\propto\frac {1}{R}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html: An a.c. source generating a voltage v = vmsin at is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C. Find the expression for the current i, flowing through it. Plot a graph of v and i versus at to show that the current is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} ahead of the voltage. What is capacitive reactance.
question_text: An a.c. source generating a voltage v = v m sin at is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C. Find the expression for the current i, flowing through it. Plot a graph of v and i versus at to show that the current is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} ahead of the voltage. What is capacitive reactance.
explanation: V = V o sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{Q}}{d t}{/tex} I o = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}\right)}{/tex} I = I o sin {tex}\left(\frac{(1) t+\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Capacitive reactance is defined as the resistance offered to the flow of current by the capacitor. It is denoted by the letter X L
solution: V = V o sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{Q}}{d t}{/tex} I o = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}\right)}{/tex} I = I o sin {tex}\left(\frac{(1) t+\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Capacitive reactance is defined as the resistance offered to the flow of current by the capacitor. It is denoted by the letter X L
solution_html:
V = Vo sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t V = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex}
I = {tex}\frac{d \mathrm{Q}}{d t}{/tex}
Io = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{\left(\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}\right)}{/tex}
I = Io sin {tex}\left(\frac{(1) t+\pi}{2}\right){/tex}

Capacitive reactance is defined as the resistance offered to the flow of current by the capacitor. It is denoted by the letter XL

subject: Physics
question_html: The instantaneous current and voltage of an a.c circuit are given by i = 10 sin (314t) and v = 50 sin (314t + {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}). What is the power dissipation in the circuit?
question_text: The instantaneous current and voltage of an a.c circuit are given by i = 10 sin (314t) and v = 50 sin (314t + {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} ). What is the power dissipation in the circuit?
explanation: Given, i = 10 sin 314t v = 50 sin (314t + {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} i 0 = 10 A, V 0 = 50 V and {tex}\phi = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Power dissipation in the circuit is given by {tex}P = {E_v}{I_v}\cos \phi{/tex} {tex}{E_v} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }},{V_0} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} {tex}P = \left( {\frac{{50}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\left( {\frac{{10}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\cos \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex}
solution: Given, i = 10 sin 314t v = 50 sin (314t + {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} i 0 = 10 A, V 0 = 50 V and {tex}\phi = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Power dissipation in the circuit is given by {tex}P = {E_v}{I_v}\cos \phi{/tex} {tex}{E_v} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }},{V_0} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} {tex}P = \left( {\frac{{50}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\left( {\frac{{10}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\cos \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, i = 10 sin 314t
v = 50 sin (314t + {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex})
{tex}\therefore{/tex} i0 = 10 A, V0 = 50 V and {tex}\phi = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
Power dissipation in the circuit is given by
{tex}P = {E_v}{I_v}\cos \phi{/tex}
{tex}{E_v} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }},{V_0} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
{tex}P = \left( {\frac{{50}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\left( {\frac{{10}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\cos \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\left[ {\because \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: An electrical element X, when connected to an alternating voltage source, has the current through it leading the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian. Identify X and write an expression for its reactance.
question_text: An electrical element X, when connected to an alternating voltage source, has the current through it leading the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian. Identify X and write an expression for its reactance.
explanation: In an a.c. circuit having capacitor, the current leads the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian. Therefore, the electrical element X is capacitor. The reactance of the capacitor is given by X C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex}
solution: In an a.c. circuit having capacitor, the current leads the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian. Therefore, the electrical element X is capacitor. The reactance of the capacitor is given by X C = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex}
solution_html:
In an a.c. circuit having capacitor, the current leads the voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} radian. Therefore, the electrical element X is capacitor.
The reactance of the capacitor is given by
 XC = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \pi f C}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: How much current is drawn by the primary of a transformer which steps down 220V to 22V to operate a device with an impedance of 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex}?
question_text: How much current is drawn by the primary of a transformer which steps down 220V to 22V to operate a device with an impedance of 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ?
explanation: Here {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 220 V, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 22 V, Z 2 = 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current drawn by the secondary or by the device of impedance 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{Z_{2}}=\frac{22}{220}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} If there are no energy losses, then Input power = Output power i.e., {tex}\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}=\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}{/tex} or {tex}I_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{22 \times 0.1}{220}{/tex} = 0.01 A
solution: Here {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 220 V, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 22 V, Z 2 = 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current drawn by the secondary or by the device of impedance 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{Z_{2}}=\frac{22}{220}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} If there are no energy losses, then Input power = Output power i.e., {tex}\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}=\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}{/tex} or {tex}I_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{22 \times 0.1}{220}{/tex} = 0.01 A
solution_html:
Here {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 220 V, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 22 V, Z2 = 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Current drawn by the secondary or by the device of impedance 220 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{Z_{2}}=\frac{22}{220}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
If there are no energy losses, then
Input power = Output power i.e., {tex}\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}=\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}{/tex}
or {tex}I_{1}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{22 \times 0.1}{220}{/tex} = 0.01 A

question_html: The instantaneous current and voltage of an a.c. circuit are given by i = 10 sin 314t A and v = 50 sin 314t V. What is the power dissipation in the circuit?
question_text: The instantaneous current and voltage of an a.c. circuit are given by i = 10 sin 314t A and v = 50 sin 314t V. What is the power dissipation in the circuit?
explanation: {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{v_{0} i_{0}}{2} \cos \phi=\frac{50 \times 10}{2} \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} = 250 W
solution: {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{v_{0} i_{0}}{2} \cos \phi=\frac{50 \times 10}{2} \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} = 250 W
solution_html: {tex}P_{a v}=\frac{v_{0} i_{0}}{2} \cos \phi=\frac{50 \times 10}{2} \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} = 250 W

subject: Physics
question_html:
A town situated 20 km away from a power plant generating power at 440 V, requires 6000 kW of electric power at 200 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets power from the line through a 3000 - 220 V step down transformer at a substation in the town.

  1. Find the line power losses in the form of heat.
  2. How much power must the plant supply, assuming there is negligible power loss due to leakage?
question_text: A town situated 20 km away from a power plant generating power at 440 V, requires 6000 kW of electric power at 200 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets power from the line through a 3000 - 220 V step down transformer at a substation in the town. Find the line power losses in the form of heat. How much power must the plant supply, assuming there is negligible power loss due to leakage?
explanation: Resistance of two line wires, R = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} For the transformer E p = 3000 V, E s = 220 V and P = 600 kW = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 W {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current carried by line wires, I v = {tex}\frac{P}{E_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^5}{3000}{/tex} = 200 A. Power loss in the form of heat = {tex}I_v^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} R = (200) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 640000 W = 640 kW Power supplied by plant = power demand + power loss = 600 + 640 = 1240 kW
solution: Resistance of two line wires, R = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 16 {tex}\Omega{/tex} For the transformer E p = 3000 V, E s = 220 V and P = 600 kW = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 W {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current carried by line wires, I v = {tex}\frac{P}{E_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^5}{3000}{/tex} = 200 A. Power loss in the form of heat = {tex}I_v^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} R = (200) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 640000 W = 640 kW Power supplied by plant = power demand + power loss = 600 + 640 = 1240 kW
solution_html:
1. Resistance of two line wires,
    R = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 16{tex}\Omega{/tex}
    For the transformer
    Ep = 3000 V, Es = 220 V
    and P = 600 kW = 6 {tex}\times{/tex}105 W
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current carried by line wires,
    Iv = {tex}\frac{P}{E_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \times 10^5}{3000}{/tex} = 200 A.
    Power loss in the form of heat
    = {tex}I_v^2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}R
    = (200)2 {tex}\times{/tex}16 = 640000 W = 640 kW
  2. Power supplied by plant = power demand + power loss
    = 600 + 640 = 1240 kW

subject: Physics
question_html: Define power factor.
question_text: Define power factor.
explanation: The power factor is defined as the ratio of true power to the apparent power of an a.c. circuit. It is equal to the cosine of the phase angle between current and voltage in the a.c. circuit. It is given by {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{\text { True power }}{\text { Apparent power }}=\frac{P_{a v}}{V_{r m s} I_{r m s}}{/tex} For a series LCR-circuit, power factor is {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}
solution: The power factor is defined as the ratio of true power to the apparent power of an a.c. circuit. It is equal to the cosine of the phase angle between current and voltage in the a.c. circuit. It is given by {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{\text { True power }}{\text { Apparent power }}=\frac{P_{a v}}{V_{r m s} I_{r m s}}{/tex} For a series LCR-circuit, power factor is {tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The power factor is defined as the ratio of true power to the apparent power of an a.c. circuit. It is equal to the cosine of the phase angle between current and voltage in the a.c. circuit.
It is given by
{tex}\cos \phi=\frac{\text { True power }}{\text { Apparent power }}=\frac{P_{a v}}{V_{r m s} I_{r m s}}{/tex}
For a series LCR-circuit, power factor is
{tex}\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Calculate the current drawn by the primary of a transformer, which steps down 200 V to 20 V to operate a device of resistance 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Assume the efficiency of the transformer to be 80%
question_text: Calculate the current drawn by the primary of a transformer, which steps down 200 V to 20 V to operate a device of resistance 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Assume the efficiency of the transformer to be 80%
explanation: Here {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 20 V, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 200 V, R 2 = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , {tex}\eta{/tex} = 80% Current flowing through secondary, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{R_{2}}=\frac{20}{20}=1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Efficiency, {tex}\eta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Power output }}{\text { Power input }}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{80}{100}=\frac{20 \times 1}{200 \times I_{1}}{/tex} or I 1 = 0.125 A
solution: Here {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 20 V, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 200 V, R 2 = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , {tex}\eta{/tex} = 80% Current flowing through secondary, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{R_{2}}=\frac{20}{20}=1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Efficiency, {tex}\eta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Power output }}{\text { Power input }}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{80}{100}=\frac{20 \times 1}{200 \times I_{1}}{/tex} or I 1 = 0.125 A
solution_html:
Here {tex}\varepsilon_{1}{/tex} = 20 V, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 200 V, R2 = 20 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, {tex}\eta{/tex} = 80%
Current flowing through secondary,
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2}}{R_{2}}=\frac{20}{20}=1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
Efficiency, {tex}\eta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { Power output }}{\text { Power input }}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{80}{100}=\frac{20 \times 1}{200 \times I_{1}}{/tex}
or I1 = 0.125 A

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the maximum value of current when an inductance of one henry is connected to an a.c. source of 200 volts, 50 Hz
question_text: Find the maximum value of current when an inductance of one henry is connected to an a.c. source of 200 volts, 50 Hz
explanation: Given, inductance of the inductor, L = 1H, voltage (rms) of the source, V = 200V, frequency of the source, f = 50Hz. Now, inductive reactance X L = {tex}\omega L{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL, and, maximum voltage V 0 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200V, Hence, maximum current I 0 = {tex}\frac{V_0}{X_L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2\times200}{2\pi fL}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{200}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 0.9A.
solution: Given, inductance of the inductor, L = 1H, voltage (rms) of the source, V = 200V, frequency of the source, f = 50Hz. Now, inductive reactance X L = {tex}\omega L{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} fL, and, maximum voltage V 0 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200V, Hence, maximum current I 0 = {tex}\frac{V_0}{X_L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2\times200}{2\pi fL}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{200}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 0.9A.
solution_html:
Given,
inductance of the inductor, L = 1H,
voltage (rms) of the source, V = 200V,
frequency of the source, f = 50Hz.
Now,
inductive reactance XL = {tex}\omega L{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}fL,
and, maximum voltage V0 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 200V,
Hence, maximum current I0 = {tex}\frac{V_0}{X_L}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2\times200}{2\pi fL}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{200}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 0.9A.

question_text: A 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} F capacitor is connected to a 220 V-50 Hz a.c. source. Find the virtual value of current through circuit. What is the peak value of voltage across the capacitor?
explanation: Here C = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 E V = 220V, v = 50 Hz I v = E V /X C = E V /1/wC = E V (wC) = E v (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vC) I = 220 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{22}{7}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 = 0.207A E O = {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} E v = 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 311.1V
solution: Here C = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 E V = 220V, v = 50 Hz I v = E V /X C = E V /1/wC = E V (wC) = E v (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vC) I = 220 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{22}{7}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 = 0.207A E O = {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} E v = 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 311.1V
solution_html:

EV = 220V, v = 50 Hz

= Ev(2{tex}\pi{/tex}vC)
I = 220 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{22}{7}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6
= 0.207A
EO = {tex}\sqrt2{/tex}Ev = 1.414 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 311.1V

subject: Physics
question_html: Give two disadvantages of transmitting a.c. over long distances at low voltage and high current.
question_text: Give two disadvantages of transmitting a.c. over long distances at low voltage and high current.
explanation: Following are the disadvantages of transmitting electrical power at low voltage: Large lengths of transmission cables have sufficient resistance. Hence a large amount of energy (I 2 Rt) will be lost as heat during transmission. A large voltage drop (IR) occurs along the line wire. Hence the voltage at the receiving station will be much smaller than that at the generating station.
solution: Following are the disadvantages of transmitting electrical power at low voltage: Large lengths of transmission cables have sufficient resistance. Hence a large amount of energy (I 2 Rt) will be lost as heat during transmission. A large voltage drop (IR) occurs along the line wire. Hence the voltage at the receiving station will be much smaller than that at the generating station.
solution_html:
Following are the disadvantages of transmitting electrical power at low voltage:

  1. Large lengths of transmission cables have sufficient resistance. Hence a large amount of energy (I2Rt) will be lost as heat during transmission.
  2. A large voltage drop (IR) occurs along the line wire. Hence the voltage at the receiving station will be much smaller than that at the generating station.
